title: Fix session pipeline: message duplication + pipe ENOENT

close_reason: Fixed: work-assigner uses in-memory boolean lock to prevent concurrent act() calls
description: In adjutant-core.ts, dispatchEvent() calls behavior.act() as fire-and-forget (line 101: behavior.act(...).catch(...)). There is no await and no serialization between concurrent act() invocations. This creates a race condition for work-assigner: (1) Two agents go idle simultaneously, emitting two agent:status_changed events. (2) Both events trigger work-assigner. Both shouldAct() calls pass the 30s debounce check. (3) Both act() calls read the same bead list and try to assign the same highest-priority bead. (4) Result: two bd update --assignee calls for the same bead, or the second overwrites the first. Similarly, the 5-minute cron tick can race with an event-triggered run. The debounce (stored as setMeta in SQLite) might prevent some races but the check-then-act pattern is inherently non-atomic — shouldAct reads the timestamp, but the timestamp isn't updated until act() completes the assignment. Fix: Add a per-behavior mutex or in-flight lock so only one work-assigner act() runs at a time. The stale-agent-nudger uses an in-memory Map for debounce which also would not protect against this.
title: Bug: Race condition — concurrent act() calls can double-assign beads

close_reason: Fixed in commit 206e7f6: Replaced broken SSE EventSource with WebSocket via CommunicationContext.subscribeTimeline. Real-time timeline events now flow through existing WS connection. Build clean, all tests pass.
description: useTimeline.ts uses EventSource('/api/events') listening for 'timeline_event' SSE events. But the SSE endpoint only forwards EventBus events via EVENT_TYPE_MAP, which does NOT include timeline_event. Timeline events are broadcast exclusively via WebSocket wsBroadcast(). Therefore real-time updates in the web timeline are broken — no events will appear without a manual refresh. Fix: subscribe to WebSocket timeline_event messages via CommunicationContext instead of SSE. This violates FR-004 and SC-003.
title: Bug: useTimeline subscribes via SSE instead of WebSocket — no real-time updates

title: Agent Personas & Roles

close_reason: Addressed: architecture_focus and modular_architecture are intentionally distinct traits - architecture_focus covers high-level system design while modular_architecture covers implementation-level separation of concerns. The prompt generator handles them as separate cognitive categories (Engineering vs Craft).
description: The trait table defines both 'architecture_focus' (System design, dependency management, clean abstractions) and 'modular_architecture' (Separation of concerns, clean interfaces, composability). These overlap significantly - clean abstractions and separation of concerns are conceptually the same thing, and dependency management is a form of composability. A user allocating points will be confused about where architecture-related points should go. Either merge these traits, or sharpen the descriptions to eliminate overlap. Suggested fix: 'architecture_focus' should be about high-level system design (service boundaries, layer definitions, technology choices), while 'modular_architecture' should be specifically about code-level modularity (small functions, interface extraction, dependency injection patterns).
title: QA: Spec gap - architecture_focus vs modular_architecture trait overlap

close_reason: Addressed: migration 007-personas.sql uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS with traits as TEXT (JSON string). The persona-service validates JSON structure via Zod on read/write. Existing SQLite columns are not affected.
description:
The plan says traits are stored as a JSON column in SQLite. Implementation concerns:

1. SQLite JSON support: SQLite has json functions (json_extract, json_type) but they require compilation with ENABLE_JSON1. The better-sqlite3 package used by this project includes this by default, but the migration should use TEXT type with application-level JSON parsing, not SQLite json functions, for portability.

2. Schema validation on read: When reading a persona from the DB, the JSON traits string must be parsed and validated against the Zod schema. A malformed JSON string (from manual DB editing or corruption) should produce a clear error, not crash the service.

3. Default trait values: When new traits are added in future versions (spec edge case: 'Existing personas retain values; new traits default to 0'), the read path must merge stored traits with the current trait definitions, filling in 0 for any missing keys. This is application logic, not migration logic, but it's not mentioned in any task.

4. Migration idempotency: The migration system uses a migrations tracking table and skips already-applied migrations (per database.ts). The 007-personas.sql migration must use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS to be safe.

These should be explicit requirements in adj-033.1.2 (migration) and adj-033.2.1 (service).
title: QA: Spec gap - Migration must handle JSON traits column safely

close_reason: Addressed: Phase 4 uses SessionStart hook with compact matcher instead of PreCompact
description:
The plan says persona prompts are injected via SessionStart and PreCompact hooks that 'call the API to fetch the persona prompt'. This has multiple problems:

1. SessionStart hooks output text to stdout which becomes 'additionalContext' for Claude - it does NOT become a system prompt. It's just user-visible context that Claude can choose to ignore. This is fundamentally different from a system prompt injection.

2. The plan says hooks live in 'backend/src/services/persona-hooks.ts' but hooks are shell scripts configured in .claude/settings.json, not TypeScript services. The hook must be a bash script that curls the API.

3. PreCompact hooks receive trigger and custom_instructions but have NO decision control and NO additionalContext output. The stdout of a PreCompact hook is NOT injected into the conversation. PreCompact is fire-and-forget for side effects only. After compaction, a SessionStart hook fires with matcher 'compact', which IS the right place to re-inject context.

4. The per-session hook registration is unclear. Hooks are configured in .claude/settings.json (project-level or user-level). You can't dynamically register hooks per-session via the API. The hook script needs to be a universal script that resolves the current agent's persona by reading ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID env var, then fetching the persona from the API.

Recommended fix: Use a single SessionStart hook (configured in .claude/settings.json) that runs on both 'startup' and 'compact' matchers. The hook reads ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID, curls GET /api/agents/:id/persona (new endpoint needed), and outputs the persona prompt to stdout. Drop the PreCompact hook entirely. The initial spawn-time injection via claudeArgs --prompt is still needed as the primary injection point.
title: QA: Spec gap - Hook injection strategy is architecturally wrong

close_reason: Addressed: Phase 4 implements ADJUTANT_PERSONA_ID env var for persona-to-agent association
description:
The spec describes spawning an agent with a personaId, but there's no mechanism to persist which persona an agent is using. The spawn route accepts personaId, injects the prompt, and forgets. This means:

1. The SessionStart hook has no way to know which persona the agent was spawned with (ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID maps to callsign, not persona).
2. If the persona is updated while an agent is running, the hook can't re-fetch the right persona.
3. The acceptance scenario 'Given persona Sentinel updated while agent zeratul runs, when next compaction occurs, then the latest persona prompt is fetched and injected' requires knowing zeratul's persona is Sentinel.

Fix: Either (a) add a persona_id field to the session registry so the hook can look it up, or (b) set an ADJUTANT_PERSONA_ID env var in the tmux session at spawn time (like ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID is already set), so the hook script can read it. Option (b) is simpler and consistent with existing patterns.
title: QA: Spec gap - No persona-to-agent association persistence

close_reason: By design: 100-point budget is intentional to force specialization. Users can spread 8 points across 12 traits for a generalist, or concentrate for specialists. The prompt generator handles low values gracefully with brief mentions.
description:
The budget is 100 points across 12 traits (each 0-20). The spec says 5 traits at max = exactly 100. But the plan says tiers are: 0 (omitted), 1-7 (low), 8-14 (medium), 15-20 (high). 'High' starts at 15, not 20. A persona with 5 traits at 'high' minimum (15) uses only 75 points, leaving 25 for other traits. This means you CAN have 5 high traits plus light coverage on 3-4 others, which doesn't feel very specialized.

Conversely, if you want 3 traits at maximum (20), that's 60 points for 9 remaining traits, averaging 4.4 each - barely 'low' tier. The math works but the design question is: does 100 produce the right tension? Consider whether 80 would enforce more painful tradeoffs, or whether the current 100 is intentional to allow 'strong specialist with minor secondary skills'.

This is a design question, not a bug. The spec should explicitly document the rationale: 'We chose 100 because it allows X pattern while preventing Y pattern.' Without this, the implementer may second-guess the number.
title: QA: Spec gap - Budget of exactly 100 is too tight for 12 traits

close_reason: Addressed in implementation: when all callsigns are disabled via master toggle, agents must be named manually or use persona names as callsigns. The spawn flow handles this - if no callsign provided and all are disabled, persona name is used.
description:
The spec says disabled callsigns are excluded from auto-assignment (FR-011) and a master toggle disables all (FR-010). But the acceptance scenario only says 'agents must be named manually or use persona names.' What happens in these concrete scenarios:

1. All callsigns disabled, no persona specified, no explicit name: What name does the agent get? The current code falls back to 'agent' (line 99 of agents.ts). This would mean multiple unnamed agents all get 'agent', which conflicts with the name-in-use check.

2. All callsigns disabled, persona specified: Does the persona name become the callsign? What if two instances of the same persona are spawned simultaneously?

3. Is 'disabled' persistent across server restarts? The plan mentions a callsign_settings table (adj-033.2.5), but the spec doesn't mention persistence.

The spec needs explicit acceptance criteria for the 'all disabled, no persona' edge case. Suggested: return a 400 error saying 'No callsign available. Provide a name or persona, or re-enable callsigns.'
title: QA: Spec gap - Callsign toggle when ALL disabled and no persona

close_reason: Comprehensive test coverage achieved: 38 persona-service tests, 25 route tests, 29 callsign tests, 36+ prompt-generator tests, 11 hook tests, 20 spawn integration tests = 159+ persona-specific tests.
description:
The spec acceptance scenarios and the test bead descriptions miss several important test cases that should be documented:

1. Budget boundary: traits summing to exactly 100 (should succeed)
2. Budget boundary: traits summing to 101 (should fail)  
3. Budget boundary: individual trait at exactly 20 (should succeed)
4. Budget boundary: individual trait at 21 (should fail)
5. Budget boundary: individual trait at -1 or negative (should fail)
6. Budget boundary: individual trait as float/decimal (should fail - must be integer)
7. Budget boundary: all traits at 0, total budget = 0 (should succeed per spec)
8. Partial update: update only 2 of 12 traits, verify others unchanged
9. Partial update: update that would push existing total over budget
10. Delete persona that doesn't exist (should 404, not 500)
11. Name uniqueness: case sensitivity - 'Sentinel' vs 'sentinel' vs 'SENTINEL' (spec says case-insensitive)
12. Name with special characters, empty string name, very long name
13. Concurrent creation of same-named persona (race condition)
14. GET /api/personas with no personas (empty array, not error)
15. Prompt generation with all traits at 0 (should produce minimal/generic prompt per edge cases section)

These should be explicitly listed in adj-033.2.3 (PersonaService tests) and adj-033.2.4 (route tests) acceptance criteria.
title: QA: Spec gap - Missing test scenarios for implementation

close_reason: Addressed: Phase 4 wires persona into all spawn paths (agents.ts, sessions.ts, swarm-service.ts)
description:
The codebase has TWO spawn endpoints:
1. POST /api/agents/spawn (agents.ts:55) - used by iOS/web dashboard
2. POST /api/sessions (sessions.ts:108) - used for generic session creation

Both auto-assign callsigns via pickRandomCallsign(). The plan only mentions modifying agents.ts spawn route (adj-033.4.1), but POST /api/sessions also needs to accept personaId. Otherwise, sessions created via the sessions API won't get persona injection.

Additionally, the swarm-service.ts createSwarm() function creates sessions via bridge.createSession() and does NOT go through the REST routes at all. If personas should work with swarm spawns, the persona injection logic needs to be in the session creation layer (session-bridge or lifecycle-manager), not just in the route handler.

Fix: Either (a) add personaId support to CreateSessionRequest in lifecycle-manager.ts so all session creation paths support it, or (b) explicitly document that personas only work via POST /api/agents/spawn and not via swarm or session APIs.
title: QA: Spec gap - Spawn route has TWO paths, plan only addresses one

close_reason: Addressed: Phase 4 uses --prompt CLI flag injection via tmux
description:
The plan says 'inject persona prompt into the tmux session's initial prompt' and references 'prompt prepend in tmux session'. But HOW?

Looking at lifecycle-manager.ts createSession(), Claude is started with:
  claude --dangerously-skip-permissions [claudeArgs]

Claude Code supports --prompt (-p) for an initial prompt. But a persona system prompt is not a user prompt - it's instructions the agent should follow, not a task to perform. The options are:

1. --prompt 'You are Sentinel. [persona instructions]. Now, await instructions.' - This works but conflates persona instructions with the first user message.
2. --append-system-prompt - This is the right mechanism if it exists (needs verification).
3. CLAUDE.md injection - Write persona prompt to a temporary CLAUDE.md or .claude/rules/ file in the project directory. This is the most reliable mechanism since CLAUDE.md is always loaded.
4. --system-prompt flag - May not exist in Claude Code CLI.

The plan must specify the exact CLI mechanism. Using --prompt is likely the only viable option, but it means the persona prompt takes up the first user message slot, and the agent won't have an actual task to start working on unless the persona prompt includes one.

Recommended: Use --prompt with the persona instructions followed by 'Await further instructions via Adjutant messaging.' This is consistent with how the current system works.
title: QA: Spec gap - Persona prompt injection mechanism undefined

close_reason: Addressed in iOS implementation: DeployPersonaSheet pre-fills callsign with persona name. If that callsign is disabled, the user can type a custom name. The spawn API handles both persona-name and custom callsigns.
description:
The iOS spec says 'When I tap [a persona], an agent spawns immediately with the Sentinel persona prompt injected and Sentinel as its callsign.' But 'Sentinel' is NOT a StarCraft callsign - it's a persona name. The callsign system currently only assigns from the CALLSIGNS roster (44 StarCraft heroes).

This creates an inconsistency: if persona names are used as agent names, they bypass the callsign system entirely. The spawn route currently checks callsigns against active sessions for conflicts (line 86 in agents.ts), but a persona name like 'Sentinel' would pass because it's not in the CALLSIGNS array.

Questions to resolve:
1. Should persona names be treated as custom callsigns (bypassing the StarCraft roster)?
2. Can two running agents both be named 'Sentinel' if the user taps the persona twice?
3. Should the second spawn auto-suffix like 'Sentinel-2'?
4. The existing name-in-use check would catch duplicates, but what's the user-facing behavior? A 409 error? Auto-naming?

The spec must define the naming behavior when spawning via persona tap.
title: QA: Spec gap - iOS spawns personas as callsign names but callsigns may be disabled

description: ResponseCache.loadPersistedChatMessages() synchronously decodes up to 50 messages via JSONDecoder on main thread (est. 100-200ms). Refactor to decode on a background queue/actor, publish results to main thread when ready. File: Adjutant/Core/Cache/ResponseCache.swift

description: DataSyncService.init() calls loadFromCache() and setupSSEIntegration() synchronously at startup (est. 75-150ms). Refactor so it initializes lazily — either on first access after the UI renders, or via a .task{} modifier in MainTabView. File: Adjutant/Core/Services/DataSyncService.swift
title: Defer DataSyncService initialization until after first render

description: AppState.init() performs 6+ sequential synchronous UserDefaults reads (baseURL, apiKey, theme, overseerMode, voiceMute, communicationPriority) totaling est. 150-300ms. Refactor to read all keys in a single Task.detached block and publish via @Published on MainActor when ready. File: Adjutant/Core/State/AppState.swift

Create AppStateBootstrap with only URL+key (needed for first API call), defer theme/voice/comms/network-recovery to a separate async initialization phase. description: These three singletons eagerly initialize at launch. close_reason: By design: heartbeats are stateless (fresh MCP tool calls each cycle), Claude Code has built-in auto-compaction, and SessionStart hooks re-inject PRIME.md after compaction
description:
File: .claude/agents/adjutant.md

The agent instructions define a heartbeat procedure that calls 5+ MCP tools per cycle (list_agents, list_beads x2, send_message per stale agent, send_message to user). Each heartbeat response from the agent + tool results adds ~500-2000 tokens to the conversation context.

With hourly heartbeats, after 24 hours the conversation will have ~12,000-48,000 tokens of accumulated heartbeat data. After a few days, the agent will hit its context window limit and either:
1. Start dropping early conversation context (losing startup instructions)
2. Error out with a context length exceeded error
3. Produce increasingly degraded responses as important context is pushed out

There is no mechanism to:
- Compact or summarize previous heartbeat results
- Start a fresh conversation while preserving agent identity
- Limit the number of heartbeats before a context reset

Suggested fix:
Add a 'Context Management' section to the agent instructions:
- After N heartbeats (e.g., 12), the scheduler should respawn the agent with a fresh context
- Or: instruct the agent to use /compact or similar context management commands
- Or: design the heartbeat to be stateless (no reference to previous heartbeats needed)
title: Bug: Agent instructions have no context window management strategy

close_reason: Verified: all tool names in adjutant.md match MCP registrations exactly
description:
File: .claude/agents/adjutant.md, lines 35-38, 49-53

The agent instructions reference MCP tool names like:
- list_agents()
- list_beads({ status: 'in_progress' })
- send_message({ to: '<agent-name>', body: '...' })

These tool names must exactly match the registered MCP tool names in backend/src/services/mcp-tools/. Let me verify:

In mcp-tools/status.ts: set_status, report_progress, announce -- these match.
In mcp-tools/messaging.ts: send_message, read_messages, list_threads, mark_read -- these match.
In mcp-tools/beads.ts: create_bead, update_bead, close_bead, list_beads, show_bead -- these match.
In mcp-tools/queries.ts: list_agents, get_project_state, search_messages -- list_agents is registered here.

All tool names check out. However, the instructions show tool calls with () syntax like 'list_agents()' which is a function call syntax, not the MCP tool_use format. Claude Code uses MCP tool calls, not function calls. The agent should understand this, but it could be clearer.

More substantively: the instructions say to call list_agents() but do not specify what to do on the FIRST heartbeat when NO agents have connected yet (empty state). The agent may report 'Issues: No agents found' or may error on empty results.

Suggested fix:
Add a note in the Heartbeat Processing section:
'If no agents are connected yet, report an empty state summary rather than treating it as an error.'
title: Bug: Agent instructions reference tool names that may not match MCP registration

description: Create backend/src/services/adjutant-spawner.ts with spawnAdjutant(), isAdjutantAlive(). Uses lifecycle-manager to create tmux session. Idempotent spawning. TDD with backend/tests/unit/adjutant-spawner.test.ts.

description:
File: backend/src/services/adjutant-spawner.ts, line 50-54

The spawnAdjutant() function creates a session with name='adjutant' but does not pass claudeArgs: ['--agent', 'adjutant']. Without this flag, the spawned Claude Code process will NOT load .claude/agents/adjutant.md and will behave as a generic agent with no heartbeat instructions.

The lifecycle manager supports claudeArgs (see swarm-service.ts which uses it for --prompt), but the adjutant spawner omits it entirely.

Reproduction:
1. Start the backend
2. Observe that adj-swarm-adjutant session is created
3. The Claude Code process in that session has no agent-specific instructions

Suggested fix in adjutant-spawner.ts:
```typescript
const result = await bridge.lifecycle.createSession({
  name: ADJUTANT_SESSION_NAME,
  projectPath,
  mode: 'swarm',
  claudeArgs: ['--agent', 'adjutant'],
});
```
title: Bug: Spawner does not pass --agent adjutant flag to Claude Code

close_reason: Edge case — single-instance deployment is the norm; multi-instance guard is a Phase 2+ concern
description:
File: backend/src/services/adjutant-spawner.ts, lines 35-46

spawnAdjutant() has a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race condition. It checks if the tmux session exists, then spawns if not. If two backend instances start simultaneously (e.g., load balancer, dev restart, or pm2 cluster mode), both will:

1. Call listTmuxSessions() — both see no adj-swarm-adjutant
2. Both proceed to createSession()
3. The second createSession() will get 'tmux session already exists' error from lifecycle-manager.ts (line 119-126), which returns {success: false}
4. This is logged as a warning but is not catastrophic

However, the LifecycleManager check (has-session) is itself racy — if both backends hit tmux at nearly the same time before either's new-session completes, both could succeed, creating two tmux sessions named adj-swarm-adjutant (tmux does NOT prevent duplicate session names by default in some older versions, though modern tmux does reject this).

Severity is P3 because the lifecycle manager's own has-session check provides a second layer of defense, and tmux typically rejects duplicate names. But this should be documented or hardened with a lockfile/mutex.

Suggested fix:
Add a comment documenting this assumption, or use an advisory lockfile in /tmp to serialize spawn attempts across processes.
title: Bug: Multiple backend instances can spawn duplicate Adjutant agents
Bug: spawnAdjutant test does not verify claudeArgs are passed

description:
File: backend/src/services/session-bridge.ts, lines 106-128 and backend/src/services/adjutant-spawner.ts

On startup, session-bridge.init() auto-creates a session for the project root with name = basename(projectRoot). If the project directory is named 'adjutant' (which it IS — this is the Adjutant project), the auto-session gets name 'adjutant', which generates tmux session 'adj-swarm-adjutant' via LifecycleManager.generateTmuxName().

Then spawnAdjutant() runs and tries to create a session also named 'adjutant', targeting 'adj-swarm-adjutant'. The spawner's idempotency check on line 43 sees the session already exists and skips the spawn — but the existing session is a GENERIC Claude Code session, not one loaded with --agent adjutant instructions.

Result: The Adjutant coordinator agent never actually gets its agent-specific instructions. It's a plain Claude Code session that happens to have the right tmux session name.

Reproduction:
1. Clone the adjutant repo to a directory named 'adjutant'
2. Start the backend
3. session-bridge.init() creates adj-swarm-adjutant as a generic session
4. spawnAdjutant() sees it exists and skips
5. The 'adjutant' agent has no coordinator instructions

Suggested fix:
Either:
1. Use a distinct session name like 'adjutant-coordinator' (not matching the project dir name)
2. Or: have spawnAdjutant() check not just whether the tmux session exists, but whether it was created with the correct agent flag
3. Or: skip the auto-session creation if the adjutant agent spawn is going to happen anyway
title: Bug: Session bridge auto-session may collide with Adjutant agent tmux name

description:
File: backend/src/services/scheduler.ts, lines 70-94

The heartbeat prompt is a 13-line, ~700+ character string sent via a single tmux send-keys -l command. While tmux send-keys -l handles literal text correctly, very long strings can exceed the tmux paste buffer or input buffer limits depending on terminal configuration.

More critically, the multi-line text is sent as a single send-keys invocation. Claude Code's input handler receives all this text at once as stdin. If the Claude Code process is mid-response (e.g., still processing a previous heartbeat or user message), the injected text will appear as garbled input interleaved with any ongoing terminal output.

There is no guard to check whether the agent is currently idle/waiting-for-input before injecting the heartbeat prompt. If the agent is mid-turn processing a previous heartbeat (which could take minutes if many agents/beads exist), the new heartbeat text will be queued in the terminal's input buffer and delivered when the agent finishes — potentially causing double-processing.

Suggested fix:
1. Before sending the heartbeat, check if the agent is idle (via isAdjutantAlive + session status check)
2. Consider using a shorter trigger phrase like 'HEARTBEAT' and let the agent instructions define the full procedure
3. This also reduces context window consumption — the full procedure text is repeated every hour in the conversation
title: Bug: sendHeartbeat injects multi-line text that may exceed tmux paste buffer limit

description:
File: backend/src/services/scheduler.ts, lines 49-50

The heartbeat prompt includes a template example with literal \\n sequences:
```
send_message({ to: "user", body: "## Hourly Status Report\\n\\nActive agents: <count>\\n..." })
```

These \\n are JavaScript escaped double-backslashes in the source, so the actual string sent to tmux will contain literal backslash-n characters (\n), not actual newlines. When the AI agent reads this prompt, it may interpret \n as literal text rather than newlines, producing a status report with literal \n in the message body.

The agent instructions in adjutant.md (line 70-86) show the summary format with actual newlines, but the heartbeat prompt contradicts this by using escaped \n.

Suggested fix:
Either use actual newlines in the prompt template (join with \n as the getHeartbeatPrompt already does between array elements), or remove the body template from the prompt and just say 'Compile an hourly summary following the format in your instructions and send it to the user'.
title: Bug: Heartbeat prompt contains backslash-n literals that may confuse the agent

close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system
description:
File: backend/src/services/scheduler.ts, line 41

The heartbeat prompt starts with:
'HOURLY HEARTBEAT CHECK -- Perform the following steps in order:'

The double-dash '--' is a common Unix convention for end-of-options. While tmux send-keys -l treats everything after -l as literal text (not flags), the double-dash in the prompt text itself is harmless from a tmux perspective.

However, the prompt also contains '<agent-name>' angle-bracket placeholders (line 48). If the literal text '<agent-name>' makes it into the tmux pane, and the terminal has any HTML-like processing, the angle brackets could be misinterpreted. This is extremely unlikely in a raw terminal, but worth noting.

More concerning: the prompt contains embedded double-quote characters (line 42, 48, 50) like:
list_beads({ status: "in_progress" })

These are JavaScript-escaped quotes in the source, so the actual string contains real double-quote characters. When sent via tmux send-keys -l, double quotes are sent literally, which is correct. But the test on line 185-188 checks that the prompt does NOT contain single quotes — it does not check that double quotes are safe for tmux transmission. This is actually fine since -l handles them, but the test comment could be more explicit about why single quotes are dangerous (they would break shell escaping if -l were ever removed).

Severity: P3 — no actual bug, but the prompt sanitization test is incomplete and the rationale is undocumented.
title: Bug: Heartbeat prompt uses double-dash that may be interpreted as tmux flag

description: QA review of adj-051 Adjutant Coordinator Agent for extensibility toward Phases 2-5 of the autonomy roadmap. Contains 10 issues found during review, including 4 P2 blockers and 6 P3 improvements.
title: QA: Extensibility review — architectural issues found

description: The Adjutant agent instructions (adjutant.md) tell the agent to 'Compare each agent'\''s lastActivity timestamp against the current time' to detect stale agents. However, the list_agents MCP tool (queries.ts) returns AgentInfo which only contains: agentId, status, connectedAt, sessionId, currentTask. It does NOT include lastActivity. The CrewMember type has lastActivity (populated by enrichWithSessionData in agents-service.ts), but this data never flows into the MCP tool response. The Adjutant agent will be unable to detect stale agents because it has no timestamp data to compare. Fix: add lastActivity to the AgentInfo interface and populate it from the agents-service data in the list_agents tool handler.
title: Arch: list_agents MCP tool missing lastActivity field — stale detection impossible

close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted
description: The scheduler.ts has its own tmuxSendKeys() function that wraps execFile('tmux', ...). The project already has a tmux.ts service module with execTmux(). The scheduler should reuse the existing tmux infrastructure rather than implementing its own. This duplication means: (1) logging behavior differs (tmux.ts logs timing, scheduler doesn't), (2) error handling patterns differ, (3) future tmux changes (e.g., adding a tmux socket path) need to be applied in two places. Fix: either export a sendKeys function from tmux.ts, or refactor scheduler to use the existing tmux module.
title: Arch: Scheduler tmuxSendKeys duplicates tmux.ts functionality

description: The adjutant-spawner.ts calls bridge.lifecycle.createSession() without claudeArgs. This means Claude Code is spawned as a generic session with no agent profile. The .claude/agents/adjutant.md file exists but is never loaded because the spawner doesn't pass claudeArgs: ['--agent-prompt', 'path/to/.claude/agents/adjutant.md'] or the equivalent flag. The spawned Claude Code instance won't know it's the Adjutant agent and won't follow the heartbeat processing instructions. Fix: pass the appropriate claudeArgs to load the adjutant agent definition on spawn.
title: Arch: Spawner does not pass --agent flag — Adjutant agent has no identity

close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted
description: The tmux session name 'adj-swarm-adjutant' is defined in two places: adjutant-spawner.ts derives it as `adj-swarm-${ADJUTANT_SESSION_NAME}`, while scheduler.ts hardcodes it as a string literal. If the session name convention changes (e.g., adj-051.4.2 changes how the session is named), one file will be updated but the other may be missed. For Phase 2+ extensibility, this constant should be exported from a single source (spawner) and imported by scheduler. Fix: export ADJUTANT_TMUX_SESSION from adjutant-spawner.ts and import it in scheduler.ts.
title: Arch: Duplicated ADJUTANT_TMUX_SESSION constant across scheduler and spawner

close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted
description: The scheduler.ts module manages a single cron job via module-level state (let cronTask). It has no mechanism to register additional scheduled tasks. For Phase 2 (auto-assignment), Phase 3 (health check), and Phase 4 (build monitoring), additional periodic tasks will be needed. The current design would require either: (a) cramming all logic into the single heartbeat callback, (b) creating separate scheduler modules for each concern, or (c) refactoring to a multi-task scheduler. Option (c) is cleanest: refactor startScheduler to accept a registry of named cron jobs, e.g., startScheduler([{ name: 'heartbeat', cron: '0 * * * *', handler: sendHeartbeat }, { name: 'health-check', cron: '*/5 * * * *', handler: ensureAdjutantAlive }]). This is a P3 since it won't block Phase 2 (you can just add a second setInterval/cron), but it will accumulate tech debt.
title: Arch: Scheduler is single-purpose — not extensible for Phase 2+ cron jobs

close_reason: Phase 3 concern — spawner will be generalized when agent spawning behavior is implemented
description: adjutant-spawner.ts is hardcoded to spawn the Adjutant agent specifically. For Phase 3 (auto-spawn agents for available work), a generic agent spawner is needed. The current spawner's functions (spawnAdjutant, isAdjutantAlive) are agent-specific. A generalized API would be: spawnAgent({ name, agentProfile, projectPath }) and isAgentAlive(sessionName). The existing LifecycleManager already provides generic createSession(), so the spawner is mostly a thin convenience layer. However, the spawner adds idempotency (check-before-spawn) and error handling that would need to be replicated. Consider refactoring to a generic agent spawner that spawnAdjutant delegates to, or simply use LifecycleManager directly in Phase 3 and add the idempotency check inline. P3 because LifecycleManager already works as the generic layer.
title: Arch: Spawner is Adjutant-specific — cannot spawn arbitrary agents for Phase 3

close_reason: Phase 2-5 concern — agent instructions will be modularized as new behaviors are added
description: The adjutant.md agent instructions define Phase 1 behavior (heartbeat, stale detection, summaries). For Phases 2-5, new behaviors need to be added: auto-assignment of idle agents (P2), agent spawning decisions (P3), build result monitoring (P4), and epic lifecycle management (P5). The current monolithic .md file will become unwieldy. Suggestion: structure the instructions with clearly demarcated sections using markdown headers (## Phase 1: Heartbeat, ## Phase 2: Auto-Assignment, etc.) so each phase's instructions can be added incrementally without rewriting earlier sections. Alternatively, use a composition pattern where the spawner assembles the prompt from multiple .md files. P3 because the file is currently small and maintainable, but will need restructuring before Phase 3.
title: Arch: Agent instructions lack modular sections for Phase 2-5 behaviors

description: The scheduler starts an hourly cron job (0 * * * *) but does not fire an immediate heartbeat on startup. If the backend starts at 10:15, the first heartbeat won't fire until 11:00 — a 45-minute gap where the user has no Adjutant status report. For the stated goal of 'reducing user babysitting,' an immediate first heartbeat after spawn is essential. Fix: call sendHeartbeat() once immediately after startScheduler(), with a short delay (e.g., 30 seconds) to allow the Adjutant agent to finish initializing. Or add a startup delay + initial fire option to the scheduler.
title: Arch: No heartbeat-on-startup — user waits up to 1 hour for first report

close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted
description: The scheduler's sendHeartbeat() blindly sends tmux send-keys to the Adjutant session. If the session has died (Claude Code crashed, tmux session killed), the heartbeat silently fails and the user gets no status reports until someone notices. This is the Phase 3 health check (adj-051.3), but it's architecturally concerning that Phase 1+2 shipped without it — the entire value proposition of 'reducing babysitting' depends on reliable heartbeat delivery. The scheduler should at minimum log a warning when the heartbeat fails that's visible in the dashboard, or ideally call isAdjutantAlive() before sending. Note: adj-051.3.1 and adj-051.3.2 already cover this, but they're marked as separate phase. Consider promoting adj-051.3 to P1 since without it, the system is unreliable.
title: Arch: Scheduler does not verify Adjutant is alive before sending heartbeat

close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted
description: The agent instructions (adjutant.md) include a 'Recently Completed' section in the summary format, but the heartbeat prompt in scheduler.ts does not instruct the agent to query for recently closed beads. The heartbeat prompt only says to call list_beads with status 'in_progress' and 'open.' Without querying for closed beads (or recent closures), the 'Recently Completed' section will always be empty. Fix: add a step to the heartbeat prompt to query for recently closed beads, e.g., list_beads({ status: 'closed' }) and filter by recent timestamp. Alternatively, the agent instructions could clarify that this section is populated opportunistically from what the agent remembers.
title: Arch: Heartbeat prompt has no recently-closed beads instruction

description: File: backend/src/acceptance/pattern-detector.ts, line 79. Query parameter parsing uses pair.split('=') which destructures as [key, value]. Since split only returns the first two segments, a query like ?filter=a=b produces {filter: 'a'} instead of {filter: 'a=b'}. Fix: use pair.split('=', 2) or pair.indexOf('=') + substring to preserve the full value.
title: Bug: query param parsing truncates values containing = sign

description: test-generator.ts lines 417-422: PUT generates harness.request.put() and DELETE generates harness.request.delete(). These return raw supertest Response, not the typed {status,body} wrapper used by GET/POST/PATCH. Assertions like res.body.data.status fail.
title: Bug: PUT/DELETE generate raw supertest calls, not typed harness wrappers

description: test-generator.ts line 521: escapeDoubleQuotes() only escapes double quotes via s.replace(/"/g, '\"'). If spec text contains a literal backslash before a quote (e.g. a\"quoted\"), the generated code produces \\" which is an escaped backslash + unescaped quote, breaking the JS string literal and causing a syntax error in the generated test file.
title: Bug: escapeDoubleQuotes doesn't escape backslashes in generated test strings

close_reason: By design: clearSteps() is for test isolation. In production, steps stay registered. Module cache behavior is expected.
description: step-registry.ts uses module-level mutable array. common-steps.ts registers at import time. clearSteps() resets but cached module won't re-register. Test suites that clear and re-import lose common steps permanently.
title: Bug: step registry global state leaks between test suites

close_reason: Duplicate of adj-058.4 (already fixed by engineer-2 with descriptionFromThen)
description: test-generator.ts generateItDescription(): strips leading pronouns then prepends 'should'. Result: 'should only pending proposals are returned' instead of 'should return only pending proposals'. The grammar fix only handles 'is/are' at start but not deeper in the sentence. See generated file line 42: it('should only pending proposals are returned sorted by newest first'). adj-058.4 may already cover this.
title: Bug: generated test descriptions are ungrammatical (should + conjugated verb)

description: reporter.ts line 65: padRight(report.featureName, BOX_WIDTH - 22) truncates names longer than 30 chars. This means the header line can be shorter than BOX_WIDTH, while the border is exactly BOX_WIDTH, causing misaligned box drawing. The summary line uses padRight(summaryText, BOX_WIDTH - 3) which also truncates long summaries.
title: Bug: reporter padRight truncates long feature names, breaking box alignment

close_reason: Edge case only affects worktrees/symlinks. Works correctly in all standard usage. Risk too low to justify complexity.
description: cli.ts line 232: isEntryPoint uses import.meta.url.endsWith(process.argv[1].replace(...)) which compares a file:// URL suffix to a filesystem path. Fails when: (1) the script path resolves through a symlink, (2) running from a git worktree where import.meta.url path differs from argv[1], (3) Windows paths (though not relevant here). Could use fileURLToPath() for accurate comparison.
title: Bug: CLI isEntryPoint detection fragile with worktrees and symlinks

description: When generating tests from specs/029-agent-personas, scenarios with 'Given a persona exists' are detected as precondition type 'none' (not 'proposal'/'agent'). So resolvePath doesn't substitute :id. Generated code: harness.get('/api/personas/:id') and harness.request.delete('/api/personas/:id') -- these send literal :id as the URL, not an actual ID. The precondition detector needs patterns for persona/generic entity types beyond proposal/message/agent.
title: Bug: generated persona tests use literal :id in path instead of seeded variable

close_reason: Low risk: api-testable classification has higher priority than ui-only, so false positives on 'views'/'filters' only matter when there's no API pattern — rare and non-blocking.
description: pattern-detector.ts line 247: UI_KEYWORDS includes 'views?' and 'filters?' which match common API/data terms. Example: 'the user views proposals' -> UI. But 'GET /api/proposals is called and views are returned' would also match 'views', incorrectly classifying an API scenario as ui-only. Similarly, 'filters' in 'GET /api/items?filters=active' would match. Since api-testable has higher priority, this mostly manifests when there's no API pattern in When but Then/Given mentions 'views' or 'filters'.
title: Bug: UI_KEYWORDS regex false-positive on 'views' and 'filters' in non-UI contexts

description: File: backend/src/acceptance/test-generator.ts, line 534. resolvePath() uses path.replace(':id', '${seeded.id}') which only replaces the FIRST :id occurrence. Paths with multiple named params like /api/beads/:id/children/:childId will have :childId left as a literal string in generated code, producing non-functional tests. Should use replaceAll or a regex to handle all :paramName patterns.
title: Bug: resolvePath only replaces first :id, leaves other path params as literals

close_reason: Acceptable — each bead rebalance is a distinct coordinator action worth logging. N events for N beads is correct granularity.
description: In coordination.ts line 414, logDecision is called inside a for-loop for each bead being rebalanced. When the bridge connects logDecision to eventStore.insertEvent, a rebalance of 10 beads will emit 10 separate coordinator:action events, 10 WebSocket broadcasts, and 10 timeline entries. This creates unnecessary noise in the timeline and wastes bandwidth. Consider: (a) batching the rebalance into a single decision entry with all bead IDs in the detail field, or (b) having the bridge deduplicate rapid-fire decisions from the same action.
title: EDGE CASE: rebalance_work emits N events per bead — timeline noise for bulk operations

close_reason: Acceptable by design — decisions table is the source of truth with 30d retention, events table is the display layer with 7d retention. Different purposes.
description: After the bridge is connected, every logDecision call will write to BOTH adjutant_decisions table (via state-store.ts line 174-177) AND the events table (via eventStore.insertEvent). This creates dual storage with no referential link between them. The adjutant_decisions table has configurable pruning (pruneOldDecisions), while the events table prunes at 7 days (pruneOldEvents). The tables have different schemas (decisions has outcome/outcome_at fields, events has messageId/beadId). Questions to resolve: (1) Should adjutant_decisions be deprecated in favor of events table with a coordinator_action eventType? (2) Should the events row reference the decision ID? (3) Are the different retention policies intentional or a bug?
title: DESIGN: Dual storage of coordinator decisions in adjutant_decisions AND events tables

close_reason: False positive — engineer-2 already added both the filter chip and event card config
description: TimelineFilters.tsx (line 21-28) hardcodes EVENT_TYPES array without a coordinator_action entry. Users cannot filter by coordinator actions in the UI. Similarly, TimelineEventCard.tsx (line 12-19) EVENT_TYPE_CONFIG lacks a coordinator_action entry — coordinator events will render with the fallback magnifying glass icon and dim color instead of a meaningful label/icon. Both files need a coordinator_action entry added.
title: BUG: Frontend missing coordinator_action filter chip and event card config

description: In backend/src/index.ts line 242-243, the EventBus onAny handler feeds ALL events into signalAggregator.ingest(). When coordinator:action events are emitted, they hit the default case in signal-aggregator.ts classify() (line 101-103) and are classified as context signals. These accumulate in the context buffer and are included in the situation prompt via snapshot(). This means the adjutant's own actions appear as context signals in its next wake, creating unnecessary noise and potential for the adjutant to react to its own actions. coordinator:action should either be filtered out before ingestion, or the classify() function should return a new 'ignore' classification for it.
title: BUG: Signal aggregator feedback loop — coordinator:action events feed back into adjutant context

description: In backend/src/index.ts line 245, stimulusEngine.triggerWatch(event, data) is called for ALL EventBus events including coordinator:action. If the adjutant registers a watch_for with event='coordinator:action' (or any wildcard-like pattern), it would trigger on its own actions, causing an immediate or cooldown-delayed wake. This is a potential infinite loop: adjutant acts -> coordinator:action event -> watch triggers -> adjutant wakes -> adjutant acts again. The cooldown (90s) prevents true runaway but still wastes tokens. Fix: either filter coordinator:action from triggerWatch, or document that watch_for should never target coordinator:action.
title: BUG: Stimulus engine triggerWatch fires on coordinator:action — adjutant can wake on its own actions

close_reason: False positive — engineer-1's bridge uses callerAgent from getAgentBySession, falling back to adjutant-coordinator
description: InsertEventInput requires agentId as a non-optional string (event-store.ts line 13). However, DecisionEntry (state-store.ts line 16-25) has no agentId field — only behavior, action, target, reason. When engineer-1 bridges logDecision to eventStore.insertEvent, they must decide what agentId to use. For coordination tools (coordination.ts), the caller is always adjutant-coordinator. But logDecision is also called from background behaviors: self-improver (behavior: 'self-improver'), health-monitor (behavior: 'health-monitor'), agent-lifecycle (behavior: 'agent-lifecycle'), memory-collector (behavior: 'memory-collector'), session-retrospective (behavior: 'session-retrospective'), stimulus-engine (index.ts:280). These have no MCP session to resolve. The bridge must either: (a) hardcode agentId='adjutant-coordinator' for all decisions, or (b) use the behavior name as agentId, or (c) use a sentinel value like 'system'. The choice affects timeline filtering — if all use 'adjutant-coordinator', filtering by that agent shows everything including background behaviors mixed with manual coordinator actions.
title: EDGE CASE: agentId field ambiguity when bridging logDecision to eventStore.insertEvent

description:
The frontend and iOS use different boundary logic for context usage color thresholds, causing inconsistent colors at exact boundary values (50%, 75%, 90%).

**Frontend (SwarmAgentCard.tsx getContextColor):**
- `pct > 90` -> red, `pct > 75` -> amber, `pct > 50` -> yellow-green, else -> green
- So: 50=green, 75=yellow-green, 90=amber

**iOS (AgentRowView.swift contextColor):**
- `<50` -> green, `50..<75` -> warning, `75..<90` -> orange, `>=90` -> red
- So: 50=warning, 75=orange, 90=red

At pct=50: frontend=green, iOS=yellow. At pct=75: frontend=yellow-green, iOS=orange. At pct=90: frontend=amber, iOS=red.

**Fix:** Align one to match the other. The iOS ranges are arguably more intuitive (50 means "half full" so should be warning). Recommend changing frontend to use `>=` thresholds: `pct >= 90` red, `pct >= 75` amber, `pct >= 50` yellow-green, else green.
title: iOS/frontend context color thresholds are off-by-one at boundaries

title: QA findings: cost tracking edge cases and bugs

description: getBurnRate() in cost-tracker.ts queries SUM(total_cost) across agent_costs rows within time windows. But total_cost is the cumulative session cost, not a delta. If a session at $5.00 total gets 3 upserts in 10 minutes, burn rate sees $15 instead of the actual $5. This produces wildly inflated burn rate numbers. Fix: either store deltas per row, or track the cost at the start of each window and subtract.
title: Bug: burn rate query sums cumulative totals instead of deltas

description: After engineer-1's delta tracking changes, total_cost in agent_costs rows is a DELTA (cost for one bead segment), not the cumulative session total. But loadCacheFromDb() still loads it directly as the CostEntry.cost value. On backend restart, if a session worked on 3 beads with deltas $3, $4, $5, the cache entry gets cost=$5 (latest row's delta) instead of $12 (actual session total). This means getCostSummary() and agent cards show wrong session costs after restart. Fix: loadCacheFromDb should SUM(total_cost) across all non-finalized rows for a session to reconstruct the true session total.
title: Bug: loadCacheFromDb restores delta cost as session total after delta tracking

description: sessionBeadTracker (Map<string, BeadSnapshot>) is in-memory only and not restored from SQLite during loadCacheFromDb(). On backend restart, the first cost_update for a still-alive session enters the 'no tracker' branch, sets costAtStart=0, and creates a new row with delta=full-session-cost. This means the old bead's finalized delta is correct, but the new row after restart captures ALL session cost as if it belongs to whatever bead the session is currently on. Fix: during loadCacheFromDb, reconstruct sessionBeadTracker from the latest non-finalized rows — set beadId from the row, costAtStart from SUM of all prior finalized deltas for that session, and rowId from the row's id.
title: Bug: sessionBeadTracker not restored on backend restart — delta tracking breaks

description: When upsertSessionCost inserts a new row for a bead switch (line 746-761), it writes entry.tokens.input/output/cacheRead/cacheWrite which are the SESSION-wide running totals (from the Math.max() accumulation in recordCostUpdate). These are NOT per-bead token counts. So getBeadCost() reports inflated token counts for later beads: bead A gets the correct tokens from its time period, but bead B gets the full session's token totals instead of just the tokens used during bead B. Fix: track token snapshots alongside cost snapshots in BeadSnapshot, and compute token deltas the same way cost deltas are computed.
title: Bug: token counts in delta rows are session totals, not per-bead

description: checkAndEmitBudgetAlerts() in cost-tracker.ts emits budget_warning or budget_exceeded events on EVERY recordCostUpdate() call once the threshold is crossed. Unlike the flat alert threshold (which uses alertedSessions Set for dedup), budget alerts have no dedup mechanism. A session updating cost 100 times after crossing the warning threshold will emit 100 budget_warning events. This floods EventBus/WebSocket/iOS with duplicate alerts.
title: Bug: budget alerts fire repeatedly on every cost update (no dedup)

close_reason: Fixed by engineer-1's delta tracking (adj-066.1.3). New cost rows are inserted on bead change, preserving old bead's cost attribution.
description: upsertSessionCost() uses COALESCE(?, bead_id) — when an agent switches from bead A to bead B, the single row's bead_id is updated to B. Now getBeadCost('A') returns null because no rows have bead_id='A' anymore. The existing test 'should deduplicate sessions in epic cost when session spans multiple beads' (line 419-430) demonstrates this: it asserts totalCost=15.0 (the session total on bead B), but bead A's $10.00 of work is invisible. This is the core bug that adj-066.1.3 (delta tracking) is supposed to fix — flagging so the engineers know the test assertion itself is wrong and needs updating.
title: Bug: bead_id overwritten on bead switch loses old bead cost attribution

description: loadCacheFromDb() in cost-tracker.ts queries all agent_costs rows grouped by session_id with no filter. On backend restart, every historical session (including killed/finalized ones) gets loaded into sessionCache. getCostSummary() then reports the sum of ALL historical sessions as the 'total cost', which grows forever. This makes the dashboard cost panel show an ever-increasing number that includes dead sessions from weeks ago. adj-066.2.3 should add a finalized_at filter, but the interaction with loadCacheFromDb needs to be tested carefully — if the finalized_at migration hasn't run on an existing DB, the query will fail.
title: Bug: loadCacheFromDb loads ALL sessions including dead ones — inflates summary

description: getEpicCost() deduplicates sessions by taking the max cost per sessionId across child beads. This means if session X worked on bead A (cost $5) then bead B (cost $10 total), the epic reports session X at $10 total — which is correct for the epic total but makes it impossible to answer 'how much did bead A cost?' within the epic context. After adj-066.1.3 adds delta rows, getEpicCost needs to sum deltas per bead rather than dedup by session max. Otherwise epic cost will still be session-max, not sum-of-bead-deltas.
title: Bug: getEpicCost dedup-by-max hides per-bead cost breakdown

description: killSession() in session-bridge.ts calls connector.detach() then lifecycle.killSession(). Between these calls (or even during detach), the capture-pane poll timer (1.5s interval) could fire and emit a cost_update event. The output handler in session-bridge calls recordCostUpdate() which inserts/updates SQLite. If killSession also clears the cache entry (as adj-066.2.1 plans), the sequence could be: (1) killSession starts, (2) poll fires, cost_update recorded, (3) killSession clears cache, (4) SQLite row persists but cache is empty — inconsistency. Fix: clear the interval BEFORE detaching, or use a 'killing' flag to reject cost_updates during teardown.
title: Edge case: race between killSession and concurrent cost_update

description: If an agent never calls set_status with a beadId (e.g. the user is just chatting, or the agent forgot), all cost_update events produce rows with bead_id=NULL. These rows are invisible to getBeadCost() and getEpicCost(). Over time, significant cost can accumulate with no bead attribution. There should be: (1) a way to query unattributed costs (WHERE bead_id IS NULL), (2) the cost summary/dashboard should show 'unattributed' as a category, (3) an API endpoint or tool to retroactively assign a bead_id to unattributed cost rows.
title: Edge case: agent never sets beadId — NULL bead_id rows accumulate forever

close_reason: Low priority P3 — extractStatusBarCost first-match is correct for single-session panes
description: extractStatusBarCost() in session-connector.ts iterates lines and returns on the FIRST match of the STATUS_BAR pattern (NN% followed by the prompt chevrons). In a tmux pane with split views or if the capture includes multiple status bar renderings (e.g., pane resized), only the first match is returned. If the first match is stale (from a previous render), the cost data could be outdated. Should match the LAST occurrence instead of the first. Change: iterate lines in reverse, or collect all matches and take the last one.
title: Bug: extractStatusBarCost only matches first status bar line — misses multi-session tmux

description: In session-bridge.ts killSession(), clearSessionCost(sessionId) is only called inside the 'if (killed)' block. If lifecycle.killSession() returns false (tmux session already gone/killed externally), the cost cache entry is never cleared and the finalized_at timestamp is never set. The stale session appears in getCostSummary() until the next backend restart triggers finalizeOrphanedSessions(). Fix: call clearSessionCost() regardless of whether lifecycle.killSession() succeeded — the cost data should always be finalized when we intend to kill the session.
title: Bug: killSession skips clearSessionCost when tmux session already dead

description: The API endpoint GET /api/costs/by-bead/:id exists but neither the web CostPanel nor the iOS CostOverviewWidget uses it. Business users need to see "how much did task X cost?" and "how much did epic Y cost?" from the dashboard. Add a per-bead cost breakdown view that shows cost per task within an epic, with drill-down from epic to child beads. This is the #1 missing product feature for cost reporting.
title: Product: Per-bead cost view in dashboard and iOS

This is far more granular and accurate than the statusline-scraping approach. Implement a JSONL reader that can: (1) parse session logs for historical cost calculation using Anthropic's published pricing, (2) reconcile against statusline-reported totals, (3) provide token-level attribution per API call. Use statusline for real-time display, JSONL for authoritative records.

close_reason: All 5 tasks merged to main — event-driven cost/context extraction complete, 2643 tests passing
description: Currently cost/context data is only captured when a user opens the full session terminal view (which starts tmux capture-pane polling). If the user only uses normal chat on iOS/web, the polling never starts and cost/context are never recorded. Fix: subscribe to the EventBus agent:status_changed event, and on each status change, do a one-shot tmux capture-pane for that agent's session to extract current cost and context percentage. This decouples cost extraction from the UI — costs are always tracked regardless of which view is open.
title: Event-driven cost/context extraction on agent status change

description: Create a new method extractCostOnce(sessionId) in session-connector.ts that does a single tmux capture-pane call, runs extractStatusBarCost() on the output, and returns the cost/context data without starting the full polling loop. This is the lightweight extraction path for event-driven triggers. Should reuse existing extractStatusBarCost() logic but not start startCapturePoll(). Needs the tmux session/pane reference from SessionRegistry.
title: Add one-shot cost extraction method to SessionConnector

description: Currently set_status in mcp-tools/status.ts writes to EventStore (persistent audit log) but does NOT emit on the real-time EventBus. Add getEventBus().emit('agent:status_changed', { agentId, status, beadId, task }) after updating agentStatuses. This enables other services to react to status changes without polling.
title: Emit agent:status_changed on EventBus from set_status MCP tool

close_reason: Implemented event-driven cost extraction subscriber with debouncing
description: In session-bridge.ts (or a new cost-extraction-listener service), subscribe to getEventBus().on('agent:status_changed'). Add debouncing (e.g. 2s) to avoid excessive extraction if an agent emits rapid status changes.

description: Migration 019-proposal-comments-revisions.sql creates proposal_revisions with revision_number INTEGER NOT NULL but no UNIQUE(proposal_id, revision_number) constraint. The index idx_proposal_revisions_proposal is a performance index, not a uniqueness guarantee. Without this, duplicate revision numbers could be inserted if reviseProposal implementation has a bug or future changes introduce concurrency. Fix: add UNIQUE(proposal_id, revision_number) to the CREATE TABLE statement.

description:
## Problem

When asked to generate product proposals, agent nova created spec/markdown documents instead of Adjutant proposals via create_proposal MCP tool. When nick was asked to review them, he couldn't use discuss_proposal or comment tools because no Adjutant proposals existed — only markdown files.

## Root Cause

Agents don't know that 'proposals' means Adjutant MCP proposals unless explicitly told. The word 'proposal' is generic — agents default to creating markdown documents, which bypasses the entire proposal review/comment/revision workflow.

## Suggested Fix

### 1. Add proposal creation instructions to agent spawn prompts
When spawning agents that may generate proposals, include explicit instructions:
- 'When asked to create proposals, ALWAYS use the Adjutant MCP create_proposal tool'
- 'Do NOT create markdown files as proposals — use the Adjutant proposal system'
- Include the tool signature: create_proposal({ title, description, type, project })

### 2. Add a standing order via broadcast or CLAUDE.md rule
Add to the project rules or broadcast to all agents:
'Proposals MUST be created via the Adjutant MCP create_proposal tool, not as markdown files. This ensures they appear on the dashboard, can be discussed/commented/revised by other agents, and are tracked in the proposal lifecycle.'

### 3. Consider a behavior/hook
The idle-proposal-nudge behavior already references proposals — could add a check that detects when agents create proposal-like markdown files and nudges them to use the MCP tool instead.

## Recommended Approach
Option 2 (CLAUDE.md rule) is the simplest and most durable — it survives across sessions and applies to all agents automatically. Add a section to the project rules files that agents load on startup.
title: Enforce proposal creation via Adjutant MCP — agents must not use markdown files as proposals

close_reason: Duplicate of adj-115 (same create_proposal No project context bug)
description:
## Problem

Multiple agents (adjutant-coordinator, potentially nick/nova) hit 'No project context — cannot create proposal' when calling create_proposal MCP tool. This was introduced by adj-072 (proposal project scoping enforcement).

## Root Cause

adj-072.1 added server-side enforcement: create_proposal auto-sets project from the agent's MCP session context and REJECTS if no project context exists. But not all agents have project context set in their MCP session — especially:
1. The adjutant-coordinator (Adjutant-scoped but may not have explicit project context)
2. Agents spawned for cross-project tasks
3. Agents whose sessions pre-date the project context feature

The enforcement is correct in principle (proposals should be project-scoped) but too aggressive — agents with no project context get a hard rejection instead of being prompted to specify a project.

## Observed Incidents
- Coordinator tried to create a C4 proposal and got 'No project context — cannot create proposal' — had to delegate to amon (C4-scoped)
- Nick may have hit similar issues when trying to register Nova's proposals retroactively (he was reviewing C4 proposals but his session project context is unknown)
- Nova never created proposals at all, possibly because she didn't know about the MCP tool or hit the same error

## Suggested Fix
1. When project context is missing, allow the agent to explicitly specify a project parameter in create_proposal (currently the client-supplied project is IGNORED per adj-072)
2. Fall back to requiring explicit project param rather than hard-rejecting
3. The current behavior of auto-setting from session context should remain the default — just don't reject when context is absent AND a project param is supplied
notes: User clarification: the actual issue was that no ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID env var was set for the agent. Without this, the MCP session has no agent identity, which means no project context lookup is possible. Fix should ensure agents always have ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID set, OR handle the missing env var gracefully by falling back to explicit project param.
title: Bug: create_proposal fails for agents without project context — 'No project context' error

description:
Two different versions of backend/src/db/migrations/002_storyboards.js exist across worktrees:

- agent-a3e3efc6: uses `script` column, `scene_number`, `float` duration, `uuid_generate_v4()`, `enu()` for status, includes `metadata` jsonb on both tables
- agent-a5b2e2e9: uses `script_text` column, `order_index`, `decimal(6,2)` duration, `gen_random_uuid()`, CHECK constraint for status, no metadata on storyboards

The routes (storyboards.js) and iOS models (Storyboard.swift, Scene.swift) all reference `script_text` and `order_index`, matching agent-a5b2e2e9. The a3e3efc6 migration must be discarded or reconciled before merge, or the routes will fail with column-not-found errors.

Impact: The a3e3efc6 migration also uses `uuid_generate_v4()` which requires the uuid-ossp extension (only created in 001_initial), while a5b2e2e9 uses `gen_random_uuid()` which is native to PG14+. Using the wrong one could cause migration failures on certain Postgres versions.

Files: backend/src/db/migrations/002_storyboards.js (both worktrees)
title: Bug: Divergent 002_storyboards migration in agent-a3e3efc6 conflicts with routes and iOS models

title: Bug: Uppercase file extensions (e.g. .MD, .JSON) rejected by readFile

close_reason: Fixed by adj-bfr9 — else clause added
description: In FileContentView.swift, the body uses if/else-if chaining: (1) isLoading -> spinner, (2) fileContent != nil -> content, (3) errorMessage != nil -> error banner. But there is NO else clause. If isLoading=false, fileContent=nil, and errorMessage=nil (e.g. after a CancellationError is swallowed by BaseViewModel.performAsync), the view renders a completely blank/black screen with no indication of what happened. This is the known black screen bug. Fix: add an else clause that shows either an empty state message or triggers a retry. Note: the staff-eng is reportedly fixing this, but it is documented here for completeness.
title: Bug: FileContentView shows blank screen when fileContent is nil and no error

For both views, ensure the task text is displayed for all statuses including idle/done.

close_reason: Fixed: decommissioner explicitly excludes adjutant-coordinator and adjutant agent IDs
description:
The spec for adj-052.2.4 (Agent Decommissioner) says: find agents idle 30+ minutes with no pending work, send graceful shutdown. But the Adjutant coordinator agent (`adjutant-coordinator`) itself may appear idle for extended periods between heartbeat cycles (it's a long-running session that wakes on prompts). If the decommissioner doesn't explicitly exclude it, it will send a shutdown request to the Adjutant itself, killing the coordinator.

The health-monitor behavior (health-monitor.ts) separately ensures the Adjutant is alive. If the decommissioner kills it and health-monitor respawns it, you get a decommission/respawn thrashing loop every 30 minutes.

**Impact**: P1 — killing the coordinator paralyzes the entire system. All behaviors lose their prompt-injection target, summaries stop, and work assignment stalls until health-monitor respawns it.

**Fix**: The decommissioner's shouldAct() or act() must have an explicit exclusion list containing at minimum `adjutant-coordinator` (imported from adjutant-spawner.ts as ADJUTANT_SESSION_NAME). Consider a configurable `protected_agents` list in metadata.

**Files**: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-decommissioner.ts (to be created per adj-052.2.4), backend/src/services/adjutant-spawner.ts (ADJUTANT_SESSION_NAME constant)
title: Bug: Decommissioner must exclude the Adjutant coordinator from shutdown targets

description: decommission_agent currently only sends a 'please shut down' message — it does NOT kill the tmux session or remove from the session registry. This means session slots are never freed. Fix: (1) Actually kill the tmux session and remove from registry on decommission. (2) Add a guard requiring explicit user approval before decommissioning — coordinator should not use this autonomously without user request.
title: Fix decommission_agent: hard kill + require user approval

description:
The Adjutant system has multiple layers of agents that are easily confused, leading to wrong tooling choices (e.g., using spawn_worker when native teams were needed, or subagents when real agents were needed). We need clear, consistent terminology for each layer:

1. **Coordinator** — adjutant-coordinator, the orchestration layer that directs work
2. **Adjutant Agents** — persistent tmux-managed agents (nova, nick, raynor, kerrigan, etc.) spawned via spawn_worker, visible on the dashboard, tracked by the session registry, subject to MAX_SESSIONS cap
3. **Native Claude Team Agents** — spawned by an agent using Claude Code's Agent tool with isolation: worktree. Managed by the spawning agent (team lead), NOT by Adjutant. Invisible on the dashboard. Inherit the spawner's project context.
4. **Native Claude Subagents** — spawned by an agent using the Agent tool WITHOUT worktree isolation. Run inside the spawner's process. For internal research/exploration only, not real work output.

Deliverables:
- Document the hierarchy with clear names, when to use each, and which tools spawn which
- Update CLAUDE.md rules and spawn-related instructions to use the new terminology consistently
- Update the coordinator's memory and correction records
- Consider whether the dashboard should surface native team agents (probably not for MVP)
title: Define agent hierarchy nomenclature to disambiguate spawning layers
