close_reason: Added ## Delta Since Last Nudge section to SITUATION prompts with event-based change tracking
description:
The coordinator gets inundated with identical SITUATION nudges (especially auto-develop REVIEW nudges) and becomes desensitized — leading to multi-hour stalls when actual changes happen (e.g., Duke completing reviews).

Fix: SITUATION nudges should include lightweight status deltas so the coordinator can quickly distinguish 'nothing changed' from 'something happened':
- Agent status changes since last nudge (e.g., 'duke: working→done')
- New messages from relevant agents since last nudge
- Proposal score changes since last nudge
- Bead status changes since last nudge

This doesn't need to be verbose — just enough signal for the coordinator to know whether to investigate further or dismiss. Example: 'DELTA: duke→done (2 new messages), 2 proposals scored since last check' vs 'DELTA: no changes'.
title: SITUATION nudges should include delta/change summary so coordinator knows when to act

description: selendis (adj-164.3) wired iOS chat test files into Adjutant.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj because CI xcodebuild only runs pbxproj-listed tests AND 'cd ios && swift test' fails on macOS due to an unguarded 'import UIKit' in ChatViewModel.swift. This deviates from the SPM rule (MEMORY: never add files under Adjutant/ to .pbxproj — SPM auto-discovers). Elegant fix: guard UIKit imports with #if canImport(UIKit) so SPM swift test runs on macOS, confirm the iOS chat tests run via SPM, then revert the pbxproj test-target additions to restore full auto-discovery compliance. Verify CI still gates the tests after the change.

description: adj-164.7.5: the gated Puppeteer keystroke-latency test (tests/perf/keystroke-latency.test.ts) times out against the reworked CommandChat. Root cause: it grabs the FIRST input[type=text] which is now the search bar (and the coordinator-DM default view adds a load path). It should target the message input explicitly (.chat-input) and wait for that element's value. formatter-cache + leak-overview budgets PASS; this is a test-harness maintenance gap, not a measured latency regression (keystroke path + adj-139 virtualization unchanged, 682 frontend unit tests green). Verified manually 2026-05-29.

close_reason: Fixed + merged to main; frontend suite green
description: The spec (US1, US3) explicitly distinguishes action_required (a blocking task the General must DO — provide a key, grant access, approve) from answer-type categories. The current implementation renders action_required as a cyan chip 'ACTION_REQ' with identical styling to every other category chip. There is no visual differentiation that communicates 'I need you to take an action, not answer a question'. A General scanning 20 questions needs to instantly recognize which rows require action vs. which need a decision/answer — right now both look the same. Fix: give action_required a distinct visual treatment: a different color (e.g. orange/amber — already used for 'high' urgency, or a separate accent), a stronger border, or an icon prefix (e.g. '>> ACTION_REQ') that makes it scan differently from question-type categories. File: frontend/src/components/questions/OpenQuestionsView.tsx — CATEGORY_LABELS/categoryChip styles.
title: UX: action_required category visually indistinguishable from question categories

close_reason: Fixed + merged to main; frontend suite green
description: When answer() or dismiss() throws, the hook sets the top-level error state (line 215, 226) but the QuestionRow component catches the error itself and silently swallows it (catch block comment: 'error handled in hook'). The user sees a global error banner at the top of the view (errorBar style), but has no per-row feedback: the row doesn't flash, show an inline error, or give any affordance for which action failed. On a long list this is confusing — the General does not know which row's answer failed. Additionally the submitting=false reset in 'finally' makes the row appear ready again with no indication of failure. Fix: pass an error prop back down to QuestionRow (or use a rowError local state that gets set via a thrown re-catch) so the row shows an inline 'FAILED — retry' message below the answer form. File: frontend/src/components/questions/OpenQuestionsView.tsx QuestionRow + frontend/src/hooks/useOpenQuestions.ts answer/dismiss.
title: UX: answer/dismiss error not surfaced per-row — only shown in global error bar

close_reason: Fixed + merged to main; frontend suite green
description: Blocking questions (urgency='blocking') get a left-border red color and a CSS box-shadow glow, which is good. However the rowBlocking style uses 'transition: box-shadow 0.15s ease' inherited from the row style. More critically, there is no persistent attention-demanding treatment: no pulsing animation, no sticky position, no background differentiation beyond the box-shadow. In a long list the blocking row can visually blur into high-urgency rows (both have colored left borders). The spec says 'blocking question is always at the top' (Success Criteria) but the sort is server-side — if a General has a category filter active that shows a mix of urgencies, blocking questions need a strong persistent indicator beyond a slightly redder left border. Fix: add a subtle repeating CSS animation (e.g. a slow glow pulse using @keyframes on the box-shadow, or a background-color flicker between #111111 and rgba(255,34,34,0.07)) to rowBlocking so it persists as visually distinct. File: frontend/src/components/questions/OpenQuestionsView.tsx rowBlocking style.

Collapsing it by default means the General must click [+] CONTEXT on every question before they can meaningfully answer. For a triage session with 10 questions, that's 10 extra clicks, and the framing that enables a fast, accurate answer is hidden until requested. close_reason: Fixed: applyListenerPort preserves trailing inline comment on re-pin. Regression tests RED->GREEN. Merged f26444a7.
description: cli/lib/dolt-pin.ts applyListenerPort() rewrites the matched port line as `${indent}port: ${port}` — any trailing inline comment or content is DROPPED. Repro: config.yaml line 'port: 17005  # pinned by adjutant' re-pinned to 17009 becomes 'port: 17009' (comment gone). The first pin of a clean beads template is fine (port: 49599 has no trailing comment), so this bites the live-cutover RE-PIN path (US1) and any operator-annotated config. The module's own idempotency/comment-preservation guarantee is violated for annotated lines. Fix: preserve any text after the port value (capture and re-append the trailing comment). Add a regression test for a port line with a trailing comment.
title: Bug: dolt-pin re-pin strips trailing comment on the active listener port line

close_reason: Fixed: flow-style listener detected and edited in place (no duplicate block); idempotent. Regression tests RED->GREEN. Merged f26444a7.
description: applyListenerPort()'s listener detector regex is /^listener:\s*$/, which only matches a block-style header on its own line. A flow-style 'listener: {host: 127.0.0.1, port: 49599}' is NOT matched, so a SECOND 'listener:' block is appended. YAML with two top-level 'listener:' keys is ambiguous/non-strict-valid (last-wins in most parsers, so it functionally pins, but leaves a stale conflicting block). Real beads templates use block style so severity is low, but tests cover neither flow-style nor the no-listener-append-then-re-pin (idempotency) case. Add tests: flow-style listener, and re-pin after an append must not stack multiple appended blocks.
title: Coverage gap: dolt-pin emits duplicate listener block for flow-style listener: {..}

close_reason: Fixed: path-boundary match in classify_dolt; sibling .beads-backup no longer killed. Regression tests RED->GREEN. Merged to main 8f69c49f.
description: scripts/bd-doctor.sh classify_dolt uses a glob prefix match: [[ "$_cwd" == "$BD_DIR"* ]]. A dolt server whose cwd is a SIBLING directory sharing the .beads prefix (e.g. /proj/.beads-backup, /proj/.beads2, /proj/.beads.bak) matches and is classified as a rogue under THIS project's data-dir, then KILLED. This kills a different project's healthy dolt server — the exact 'server appears down' outage adj-182 fixes, and violates the spec's 'never kill the wrong process' intent. Reproduced: BD_DIR=/tmp/qa-bd/.beads, override cwd=/tmp/qa-bd/.beads-backup, SUPERVISED_PID set -> 'ROGUE dolt detected pid=54321 ... killing rogue orphan'. The same flawed match also mis-classifies the legitimate-server path. Fix: match exact dir or path-separator boundary, e.g. [[ "$_cwd" == "$BD_DIR" || "$_cwd" == "$BD_DIR/"* ]]. Add a regression test (sibling .beads-backup must NOT be killed).
title: Bug: bd-doctor rogue-kill cwd prefix-match kills sibling-dir dolt (.beads-backup)

close_reason: Re-scoped to adj-182.2.7 (harden in Phase 2 checkDolt per QA recommendation)
description: Under externally-managed mode bd-doctor derives SUPERVISED_PID from .beads/dolt-server.pid when BD_DOCTOR_SUPERVISED_PID is unset (script lines ~135-138). If launchd is NOT actually loaded and the pidfile is stale, and a rogue dolt happens to reuse that exact PID, the rogue is classified as the legitimate supervised server (NOT killed, reported 'alive'). Reproduced: metadata externally-managed, pidfile=88888, override '88888 33333 <beadsDir>' with no SUPERVISED_PID seam -> 'Adjutant dolt server is alive: pid=88888'. Spec US2 intent is to probe the supervised PID from the launchd agent (launchctl print) rather than trust the pidfile. This is acceptable for 1.5's documented pidfile-derivation scope but should be hardened in Phase 2 checkDolt(); flagging as a coverage/robustness gap with a test for stale-pidfile/PID-reuse.
title: Coverage gap: bd-doctor supervised-PID derived from stale pidfile can mis-trust a rogue

close_reason: Fixed: pinMetadata asserts plain object; array/scalar throw clean wrapped error (no silent port drop, no raw TypeError). Regression tests RED->GREEN. Merged f26444a7.
description: cli/lib/dolt-pin.ts pinMetadata() (lines 47-59) JSON.parses metadata.json but never asserts the result is a plain object. Edge cases verified empirically: (a) metadata.json containing a JSON ARRAY ([]) -> port assignment to array['dolt_server_port'] is dropped by JSON.stringify -> pinDoltPort SILENTLY returns the env line as if success while metadata never gets dolt_server_port, leaving beads in self-managed mode (the exact ephemeral-port churn this epic exists to fix); (b) a JSON scalar (null/42) -> throws a raw TypeError that escapes the parse-only try/catch, instead of the clean wrapped 'Dolt pin: ...' error the rest of the module uses. Fix: after JSON.parse, assert (typeof meta==='object' && meta!==null && !Array.isArray(meta)); else throw new Error(`Dolt pin: malformed metadata.json at ${metadataPath}: expected a JSON object`). Add 2 tests (array, scalar). Low real-world likelihood (live metadata is always an object) but matches the epic focus on malformed-metadata error handling.
title: Review: dolt-pin pinMetadata must reject non-object metadata.json (silent port drop / unhandled TypeError)

close_reason: Fixed: empty PROJECT_ID guard before launchctl kickstart; clear error + non-zero exit. Regression test RED->GREEN. Merged to main 8f69c49f.
description: scripts/bd-doctor.sh: when metadata.json has dolt_server_port but is MISSING project_id, EXTERNALLY_MANAGED=1 (line 96-100) yet PROJECT_ID stays empty. The --restart kickstart path (line 231) then builds 'launchctl kickstart -k gui/<uid>/com.adjutant.dolt.' with a trailing dot and no id, which fails at runtime with a confusing launchctl error instead of a clear diagnosis. Verified empirically. Fix: if EXTERNALLY_MANAGED=1 but PROJECT_ID is empty, print a clear red error ('externally-managed but metadata.json has no project_id — cannot derive supervisor label; re-pin or fix metadata') and exit non-zero BEFORE attempting kickstart. Add a regression test (metadata with dolt_server_port but no project_id + --restart -> clear error, no launchctl invocation). Low likelihood (live metadata always carries project_id) — defensive hardening aligned with the epic's malformed-config focus.
title: Review: bd-doctor must guard empty PROJECT_ID before building launchctl kickstart label

description: Code review of adj-182.2.1 checkDolt(). On a not-yet-migrated (self-managed) project — every project before `adjutant doctor --fix`/`init` runs — pinnedPort/metadataPort are null, so check #1 (port pinned) FAILs with "self-managed mode — run adjutant doctor --fix", AND check #3 (server reachable) FAILs "no pinned port to probe", AND check #4 (port file matches) FAILs "no pinned port". That is three fails for a single root cause, and `runDoctor()` returns exit 1 (hasFail) on every healthy pre-migration project. Risk: trains operators to ignore doctor output, and breaks any CI/script gating on `doctor` exit code during the rollout window. Recommend: when pinnedPort+metadataPort are both null (clean self-managed state), emit ONE actionable result (warn or info, not fail) — "self-managed Dolt; run adjutant doctor --fix to adopt supervised mode" — and SKIP the dependent reachable/port-file checks rather than failing each. Keep hard FAILs for genuine inconsistency (metadataPort != pinnedPort, stale port file, rogue). Coordinate the exit-code contract with adj-182.2.2 (--fix) and 2.3 (init).
title: Review: self-managed project yields 3 simultaneous doctor FAILs for one root cause (noisy + exit 1 pre-migration)

close_reason: Fixed + merged to main (9c33c477). Tests GREEN, CLI+backend typecheck clean.
description: Suggestion from review of adj-182.2.1. The seam is named/documented as a "SQL probe (NOT the PID — #2670)", but realSqlProbe() (doctor.ts:456) and the Phase-1 entrypoint (cli/lib/install-dolt-supervisor-cli.ts:73) both implement it as a bare TCP createConnection() that resolves true on `connect`. A bare port-open is NOT a SQL handshake: a hung/wedged dolt that still holds the listening socket, or any unrelated process bound to the port, reads as "reachable" — a softer recurrence of the #2670 false-up the epic set out to kill. This is consistent across Phase 1 and 2 (2.1 faithfully mirrors the existing seam), so it is an epic-wide design note, not a 2.1 regression. Consider upgrading realSqlProbe to a minimal MySQL-protocol handshake read (read the server greeting packet / do a `SELECT 1`) so "reachable" means "actually serving SQL". Defer if the supervisor's KeepAlive+kickstart already covers wedged-process recovery; capture the decision either way.
title: Review (suggestion): TCP-connect probe can't tell a wedged-but-listening Dolt from a healthy one — epic-wide

close_reason: Fixed + merged to main (9c33c477). Tests GREEN, CLI+backend typecheck clean.
description: Suggestion from review of adj-182.2.1. realScanDoltProcesses() runs a SECOND lsof per pid (`lsof -anP -p <pid> -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN`) purely to fill DoltProcess.port, but checkDolt() keys rogue detection on cwd + pid only and never reads .port. That is one extra subprocess per discovered dolt process for dead data. Either drop the port lookup (and the field) to halve the lsof calls in the scan, or wire .port into a check (e.g. flag a dolt under our data-dir listening on a port != pinned). Pick one; don't leave gathered-but-unused data.
title: Review (suggestion): DoltProcess.port gathered by realScanDoltProcesses but never used by checkDolt — drop the extra lsof -iTCP

description: On a FIRST install (launchd agent not yet loaded), fixDolt (cli/commands/doctor.ts) reads launchctlSupervisedPid()=null, so by design it REFUSES to kill any dolt on the data-dir (correct: do not kill the wrong process). It then proceeds to install+bootstrap the supervised server on the SAME data-dir while the pre-existing rogue self-managed dolt is still running — the exact two-servers-on-one-data-dir double-open corruption the step-2 comment (line 561) warns against. Compounding it: if the rogue already holds the pinned port, installSupervisor's SQL probe (a bare TCP connect, realSqlProbe) will CONNECT to the ROGUE on that port and report verified:true — a FALSE-POSITIVE verification. initDoltSupervisor (adj-182.2.3) shares the same first-install exposure (it has no rogue-kill at all). GAP: there is no guard that (a) detects a non-supervised owner of the pinned port before declaring success, nor (b) escalates loudly/aborts when a rogue sits on the data-dir but cannot be classified. Tests cover refuse-to-kill (cli-doctor-fix.test.ts:145) and kill-before-install ordering (:203) but NOT the first-install-with-rogue path. FIX OPTIONS: probe port-owner identity (pid via lsof) and abort/escalate when the verified server is not the one we just bootstrapped; or kill rogues that hold the PINNED PORT specifically (port ownership is unambiguous) even when launchd PID is unknown. Add regression tests for both. Suites: backend/tests/unit/cli-doctor-fix.test.ts, cli-init-dolt.test.ts.
title: Bug: doctor --fix / init risk double-open on first install — refuse-to-kill + no port-owner guard lets a rogue + supervised server share one data-dir

close_reason: Fixed: execBdWithReconnect now resets doltConnectionState on the exhausted-budget path so a recurring outage re-clears the stale circuit file each episode. Regression tests added (two consecutive failed episodes + healed-endpoint recovery). Merged to main 6da75233.
description: execBdWithReconnect (backend/src/services/bd-client.ts) only resets the module-level doltConnectionState on the SUCCESS path (line 528). When an outage exhausts the attempt budget (lines 541-544 return without reset), the dead port stays in doltConnectionState.clearedPorts. On the NEXT execBd episode against the same still-dead port, line 549 guard (!clearedPorts.has(port)) is false, so seams.clearCircuitFile() is SKIPPED — the stale /tmp/beads-dolt-circuit-<port>.json is never re-cleared and bd keeps failing fast on the dead port. This is exactly the macOS-sleep/port-churn wedge the epic exists to fix: recovery WITHOUT a process restart is defeated for any recurring/multi-call outage. Proven with a probe test: episode 2 against the same dead port performs 0 circuit-file clears (expected >0). FIX: clear/reset clearedPorts (or doltConnectionState) on the exhausted-budget return, or use a per-call local Set instead of module-level state. Add a regression test: two consecutive failed episodes against the same port must each clear the circuit file. Suite: backend/tests/unit/bd-client-reconnect.test.ts.
title: Bug: bd-client reconnect leaks clearedPorts across failed episodes — stale circuit file never re-cleared on recurring outage

close_reason: dup of adj-182.2.6.r1 (flow-style behavior block) — fixed there
description:
adj-182.2.6 (cli/lib/dolt-pin.ts applyBehaviorAutocommit) handles only BLOCK-style `behavior:` headers (regex /^behavior:\s*$/). A flow-style `behavior: {autocommit: false}` is not matched, so the function APPENDS a second active `behavior:\n  autocommit: true` block. Result is a config.yaml with TWO active `behavior:` keys:

```
behavior: {autocommit: false}
listener:
  host: 127.0.0.1
  port: 17005

behavior:
  autocommit: true
```

Duplicate YAML keys are parser-undefined; Dolt may honor `autocommit: false` (last/first wins varies), silently defeating the 2.6 fix for any flow-style config. The sibling port-pin code (applyListenerPort) ALREADY defends against flow style (flowIdx branch, lines 96-111) — applyBehaviorAutocommit should have parity.

Severity P3: the SHIPPED Dolt template is block-style+commented (the default path is safe and well-tested), so this bites only operator-hand-edited or future flow-style configs. But it is a real correctness asymmetry the team explicitly guarded against on the port path.

REPRO (verified live, temp .beads dir): seed config.yaml=`behavior: {autocommit: false}\nlistener:\n  host: 127.0.0.1\n  port: 49599\n`, call pinDoltPort(beadsDir,17005) -> two behavior keys. The existing dolt-autocommit.test.ts helper activeBehaviorKeys only counts block-style headers (/^behavior:\s*$/) so it returns 1 and MASKS the dup — add a flow-style regression test.

SECONDARY (note, not separate bead): a malformed input with a COMMENTED `# behavior:` header followed by an indented active `autocommit: false` orphan leaves TWO active autocommit lines after the append. This is garbage-in (invalid YAML) and the real template never produces it, so lower concern — but a defensive guard or a comment documenting the assumption would harden it.

FIX: add a flow-style branch to applyBehaviorAutocommit mirroring applyListenerPort's flowIdx handling (edit autocommit inside the braces, inject if absent), plus a flow-style regression test and a helper that counts BOTH flow and block behavior keys.
title: Bug: applyBehaviorAutocommit ignores flow-style behavior block, emits duplicate behavior key

close_reason: Fixed + merged to main (9c33c477). Tests GREEN, CLI+backend typecheck clean.
description: Suggestions from review of adj-182.2.6 (cli/lib/dolt-pin.ts applyBehaviorAutocommit). Two low-priority robustness gaps vs the template-only happy path (which is well covered by 8 passing tests): (1) When an active block-style `behavior:` exists but has no active autocommit line, the insert path hardcodes `  autocommit: true` (2 spaces). The REWRITE path correctly infers indent from the existing line; the INSERT path should likewise infer child indent from the block's existing children (fall back to 2) so a 4-space hand-edited block doesn't get a mis-indented sibling that misparses. (2) The block-header match `/^behavior:\s*$/` only matches block style; a flow-style active `behavior: {autocommit: false}` is missed and a second `behavior:` block is appended (duplicate key; last-wins happens to be ours so functionally OK but produces a duplicate key). This is asymmetric with the port-pin flow-style listener fix (commit 6762d909). Both are edge cases not seen in the shipped template — fix for consistency/robustness or document as out-of-scope. Add tests for each if fixed.
title: Review (suggestion): autocommit insert hardcodes 2-space indent + ignores flow-style behavior: block

description:
File: `backend/src/routes/styles.js` (on branch `003-ai-creative-director-foundation`)

The create custom style handler inserts with `is_custom: true` but never sets `user_id`. The column exists in the migration and schema but is never populated. This means:

1. Custom presets have no owner — any user can edit or delete any custom preset
2. The GET /api/styles list query filters `is_custom = false OR user_id IS NULL`, so custom presets with null user_id are always visible to everyone
3. PUT and DELETE only check `is_custom` flag, not ownership

While there's no auth system yet, this is a design gap that will cause authorization issues when auth is added. The user_id should at minimum be accepted in the request or derived from auth context.

**Fix:** Either:
- Accept user_id in the POST body and set it on insert
- Add a TODO/comment documenting this as an auth-dependent feature
- Add ownership checks to PUT/DELETE when user_id is present
title: [Bug] POST /api/styles never sets user_id — custom presets have no ownership

description: In MarkdownTextView.swift, the inlineText() and inlineElement() functions build Text views with font but do NOT set .foregroundColor for the .text case (line 145-146). The text color is inherited from the parent view's foregroundColor. In FileContentView.swift, when rendering markdown content (line 29: MarkdownTextView(content.content)), there is no .foregroundColor modifier applied. The parent Group has no foregroundColor set either. The color ultimately depends on the SwiftUI view hierarchy — which in the file content context may result in the default label color (white/black depending on appearance) rather than the theme's green text. The plain text branch (line 34) correctly sets .foregroundColor(theme.bright), but the MarkdownTextView branch does not. This could result in markdown files displaying with wrong text color. Note: the preview at bottom of MarkdownTextView.swift sets .foregroundColor(CRTTheme.ColorTheme.pipboy.primary) explicitly, suggesting this is a known requirement that the file content caller omits.
title: Bug: MarkdownTextView inline text missing foregroundColor — inherits parent

close_reason: Duplicate of adj-2jy4u (same WS sync/replay non-member leak) — already in_progress with karass. Independent QA confirmation noted.
description:
EPIC adj-164. CRITICAL room-isolation bypass on the WS recovery path.

ROOT CAUSE: wsBroadcastToConversation() (backend/src/services/ws-server.ts:778-800) fans out room-scoped (member AND subscribed) for LIVE delivery — correct. BUT it also pushes the room message into the GLOBAL replayBuffer via addToReplay(msg) at ws-server.ts:793. handleSync() (ws-server.ts:344-356) returns EVERY replay entry with seq > lastSeqSeen to ANY authenticated client with NO membership and NO subscription check. A non-member that sends {type:'sync', lastSeqSeen:0} receives all channels' messages (id + body) in sync_response.missed.

This defeats FR-007 (WS delivery MUST be room-scoped via membership) and the success criterion 'zero cross-conversation bleed' on the recovery path. The live-fan-out gate is fully bypassed.

REPRO (confirmed by QA probe, shared-checkout backend):
1. setConversationStore with chan-secret members = [raynor, kerrigan] (user NOT a member)
2. wsBroadcastToConversation('chan-secret', {type:'chat_message', id:'secret-msg', body:'members-only intel'})
3. connect+auth a client as identity 'user', send {type:'sync', lastSeqSeen:0}
EXPECTED: sync_response.missed contains NO entry with id 'secret-msg'
ACTUAL: sync_response.missed = [{type:'chat_message', id:'secret-msg', body:'members-only intel', seq:1}]

FIX OPTIONS:
- handleSync MUST filter missed entries by the requesting client's membership+subscription (resolve via conversationStore.getMembers and client.subscriptions for any entry that carries a conversationId; entries with a conversationId the client cannot access must be dropped).
- Preferred: tag replay entries with their conversationId (room messages already carry msg.conversationId) and exclude any room-scoped entry whose conversationId the client is not a member of. Non-room (global) messages remain replayable.

TEST GAP: tests/unit/ws-room-fanout.test.ts:250 only asserts replay buffering assigns a seq; no test sends sync from a non-member and asserts exclusion. Add a regression test mirroring the probe above.

FILES: backend/src/services/ws-server.ts:344-356 (handleSync), :778-800 (wsBroadcastToConversation), :793 (addToReplay).
title: QA: WS sync replay leaks room-scoped channel messages to non-members (FR-007 bypass)

close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.1.3: UserDefaults reads batched off main thread
description:
AppState.init() calls:
1. loadPersistedBaseURL() - reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'apiBaseURL')
2. loadPersistedAPIKey() - reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'apiKey')
3. Sets self.apiBaseURL and self.apiKey from the results
4. Creates APIClient with the URL and key

Then loadPersistedState() runs and:
5. Reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'selectedTheme')
6. Reads UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: 'isOverseerMode')
7. Reads UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: 'isVoiceMuted')
8. Reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'communicationPriority')
9. Reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'apiBaseURL') AGAIN
10. Creates a new APIClient AGAIN via recreateAPIClient() if URL exists

The apiBaseURL is read twice and the APIClient is created twice. Additionally, loadPersistedState() writes to sharedDefaults (app group UserDefaults) which is another I/O operation:
  Self.sharedDefaults?.set(url.absoluteString, forKey: 'apiBaseURL')

This also creates a UserDefaults(suiteName:) instance for the app group, which has its own initialization cost.

Impact: 2 redundant UserDefaults reads + 1 redundant APIClient creation + app group UserDefaults init. ~10-20ms wasted.

Suggested fix:
- Read apiBaseURL only once in init, don't re-read in loadPersistedState
- Don't recreate APIClient in loadPersistedState (it was just created in init)
- Batch all UserDefaults reads into a single method
title: Bug: AppState.loadPersistedState reads apiBaseURL from UserDefaults twice

description:
In cli/lib/checks.ts:29-36, parseJsonFile() catches all exceptions and returns null:

  try { ... JSON.parse ... } catch { return null; }

This means:
- File doesn't exist -> null (expected)
- File is malformed JSON -> null (expected)
- File exists but permission denied -> null (UNEXPECTED - masks real error)
- File is a directory -> null (UNEXPECTED)
- Disk I/O error -> null (UNEXPECTED)

Callers can't distinguish 'file doesn't exist' from 'file exists but we can't read it'. For critical files like ~/.claude/settings.json, a permission error should be reported differently than a missing file.

Consider returning a richer result: { data: T | null, error: 'not_found' | 'parse_error' | 'permission_denied' | null }

File: cli/lib/checks.ts:29-36

close_reason: P1 security leak closed: WS sync replay channel-only membership gating; full backend 3491 green. Completed by tassadar (karass+artanis stalled on worktree npm install).
description:
adj-164 code review (mohandar). backend/src/services/ws-server.ts.

wsBroadcastToConversation (line ~793) calls addToReplay(msg) for EVERY channel post, writing it into the SINGLE global replayBuffer. handleSync (lines 344-356) replays all buffered entries with seq > lastSeqSeen to ANY authenticated client, filtering ONLY by seq — no membership or subscription check. 

Impact: a non-member client that connects and sends {type:"sync", lastSeqSeen:N} receives channel chat_message bodies it is not authorized to see. This defeats the room-scoped authorization boundary that wsBroadcastToConversation otherwise enforces for live delivery. Security: cross-channel message disclosure.

The live fan-out path is well-tested (tests/unit/ws-room-fanout.test.ts) but NO test exercises sync/replay, so the leak is uncovered.

Fix options: (a) keep a per-conversation replay buffer and have handleSync re-filter by the requesting client's identity+subscriptions against conversation membership before returning entries; or (b) tag replay entries with conversationId and, in handleSync, drop conversation-scoped entries the client is not a member+subscriber of. Add a regression test: non-member sync must not receive a channel post that was buffered for replay.
title: Review: channel posts leak to non-members via WS sync/replay buffer

close_reason: Accepted P3: bd semaphore contention is bounded. Spawner runs every 10 min, work-assigner every 5 min with 30s debounce, decommissioner every 30 min. Concurrent bd calls are rare.
description:
The bd CLI is serialized through a counting semaphore (bd-client.ts, concurrency=1) because Dolt's embedded SQLite can SIGSEGV under concurrent access. Three Phase 3 behaviors all need bd access:

1. work-assigner: calls `bd ready --json` + `updateBead` (which calls `bd update`)  
2. agent-spawner: needs to check ready beads (`bd ready --json`) to decide if spawning is needed
3. agent-decommissioner: needs to check if idle agents have pending beads (`bd list`)

With the semaphore at concurrency=1, these behaviors will serialize, which is correct for safety but creates performance issues:
- The spawner's capacity check runs every 10 minutes; if work-assigner has the semaphore, spawner blocks
- Work-assigner runs on every bead:created event; if spawner is checking ready beads, assignments stall
- The 30-second debounce on work-assigner means a blocked bd call could push assignment latency past the debounce window, causing the next event to be skipped

This is not a data-corruption bug but a latency/starvation issue that gets worse as more behaviors contend for the bd semaphore.

**Impact**: P3 — latency degradation, not data corruption. But could cause visible delays in agent assignment (~seconds to minutes during contention).

**Fix options**: 
(a) Increase semaphore to 2 (risks Dolt SIGSEGV, not recommended)
(b) Have spawner cache the bd ready result from work-assigner via AdjutantState metadata (e.g., store last ready-bead count and timestamp)
(c) Accept the latency and document it as a known limitation
(d) Spawner reads from AdjutantState (in-process) rather than calling bd CLI for its capacity check

Recommend (d) — the spawner doesn't need real-time bead data, it can use the last-known ready-bead count that work-assigner already computes.

**Files**: backend/src/services/bd-client.ts (semaphore), backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-spawner.ts (to be created)
title: Bug: bd semaphore contention between spawner, work-assigner, and decommissioner

close_reason: Won't fix: documented as expected SwiftUI behavior for @StateObject init pattern
description: In FileBrowserView.swift, the @StateObject is initialized in init() using StateObject(wrappedValue:). While SwiftUI's @StateObject guarantees the object is only created once per view lifetime, the wrappedValue closure is technically evaluated on every init() call (SwiftUI discards the result after the first). This means a new FileBrowserViewModel is allocated and immediately discarded on every parent view re-render. This is a minor performance issue — the allocations are cheap but unnecessary. Best practice per Apple WWDC is to use _viewModel = StateObject(wrappedValue:) in init which is what the code does — so this is actually the correct pattern. However, if the parent view (SwarmProjectDetailView) re-renders frequently (e.g. from polling), each re-render allocates a throwaway ViewModel. Same applies to FileContentView. Low priority since SwiftUI handles this correctly, but worth noting.
title: Bug: FileBrowserView @StateObject init closure runs on every parent re-render

close_reason: Deferred P4: double-disconnect is harmless, transport cleanup handles it
description: In routes/mcp.ts, both transport.onclose (set in mcp-server.ts:135) and req.on('close') (set in mcp.ts:36) call disconnectAgent(). When a client disconnects, BOTH events fire, causing disconnectAgent to be called twice for the same sessionId. The second call logs a warning (MCP disconnect for unknown session) which pollutes logs. While not a bug (the second call is a no-op), it indicates a design issue. Either remove the req.on('close') handler and rely solely on transport.onclose, or add a guard in the route to check if already disconnected.
title: Refactor: MCP routes double-disconnect on SSE close

close_reason: Fixed: removed noise-shift animation; static SVG noise preserves CRT grain aesthetic. Matches Track E's treatment of .crt-scanlines.
description: frontend/src/components/shared/CRTScreen.css:387-403. .crt-noise has 'animation: noise-shift 0.1s steps(5) infinite' which produces a 50fps full-screen transform repaint of an inset:0 overlay at z-index:8 (covers entire CRT area). Track E (adj-139.5.1/.5.2) successfully fixed the phosphor filter and scanline animation costs, but did not touch this noise animation. The noise-shift keyframes translate the overlay by 1% in 4 step phases (transforms only, GPU-accelerated, less expensive than filter, but still constantly active over the entire viewport with z-index above content). The .crt-scan-bar at line 376 also animates continuously (6s linear) but is small (8px tall) so less costly. Spec FR-010: 'CRT visual effects MUST NOT trigger whole-screen GPU recompositing on every paint.' Recommend: slow noise-shift to 0.5s, or convert to a static SVG noise texture (the SVG is already static — only the position is animating). Verify via Chrome Rendering panel that the noise layer no longer paint-flashes on every frame.
title: QA: CRTScreen.css .crt-noise still has 50fps animation that recomposites full overlay

description: beads.ts implements a SimpleMutex class for serializing bd access. However, bd-client.ts already has a Semaphore with maxConcurrent=1 that serializes all execBd calls. The beads.ts mutex adds a second serialization layer that is unnecessary and could cause confusing deadlock if ever mixed with other code that calls execBd directly. Remove SimpleMutex from beads.ts and rely on bd-client's built-in serialization.

close_reason: Already fixed: createFromClone uses execFileSync('git', ['clone', ...]) instead of execSync with string interpolation
description: In projects-service.ts line 440, createFromClone uses string interpolation to build a shell command: execSync(`git clone ${cloneUrl} ${targetDir}`). Both cloneUrl and targetDir are user-supplied values that can contain shell metacharacters (e.g., semicolons, backticks, pipes). A malicious cloneUrl like 'https://evil.com/repo; rm -rf /' or a targetDir with shell operators would execute arbitrary commands. Fix: use execFileSync('git', ['clone', cloneUrl, targetDir]) instead of execSync with string interpolation, which avoids shell interpretation entirely.
title: Bug: Command injection in createFromClone via unsanitized execSync interpolation

description:
The init command accepts --force via bin/cli.js (commander option) and cli/index.ts (manual arg check), but:

1. The runInit function receives { force: boolean } but NEVER uses it (the stub ignores options)
2. The spec says '--force overwrites existing files' but doesn't define which files
3. The spec says 'FR-002: adjutant init MUST NOT overwrite user-modified files without --force' but there's no mechanism to detect user modifications vs generated files
4. Should --force overwrite .mcp.json? That could destroy other MCP server configs
5. Should --force overwrite ~/.claude/settings.json hooks? That could remove user hooks

The init implementation needs clear rules for what --force does:
- PRIME.md: safe to overwrite (generated content)
- .mcp.json: MERGE only, never overwrite (preserves other servers)
- settings.json: MERGE only, never overwrite (preserves user config)

File: cli/commands/init.ts:12-14, spec.md FR-002
Severity: Medium - unclear force semantics could lead to data loss
title: QA: Missing --force flag handling and documentation

close_reason: Fixed: cancel in-flight navigation task on rapid directory changes
description: In FileBrowserViewModel.swift, navigateTo() sets currentPath and fires Task { await refresh() }. But if the user rapidly taps multiple directories (e.g. dir1, dir2, dir3), three concurrent tasks run in parallel. Each calls performAsyncAction which sets self.entries on completion. The last task to complete wins, but that is not necessarily the last directory tapped — it is whichever API call returns last. This means after tapping dir1 -> dir2 -> dir3 quickly, the entries could show dir1's contents while the breadcrumb shows dir3. Fix: use BaseViewModel.startTrackedTask() which cancels the previous task, or add a generation counter. The same race exists in the view's onAppear calling refresh() while navigateTo also calls refresh().
title: Bug: FileBrowserViewModel.navigateTo race condition on rapid navigation

A THIRD copy of the loopback TCP SQL probe lives in backend/src/services/dolt-supervisor.ts realSqlProbe(). This is copy-paste that will drift (e.g. someone tunes the 1000ms probe timeout in one place only). ARCHITECTURE: the two CLI commands CAN share a new cli/lib/dolt-seams.ts (same build tree) exporting realExec/resolveDoltBin/realSqlProbe + a buildProductionInstallOptions helper. The backend service copy genuinely cannot import cli/ (rootDir:./src build boundary) so it stays separate, but should at least share the probe-timeout constant via documentation. close_reason: Fixed: work-rebalancer triggers only on mcp:agent_disconnected, not blocked status
description: The work-rebalancer spec says: 'When an agent goes blocked or disconnects, check their in-progress beads.' Treating 'blocked' the same as 'disconnected' is incorrect. An agent that reports status=blocked is still alive and working — it's indicating it's stuck on something (e.g. waiting for a dependency, permission issue, build failure). Unassigning its beads would: (1) Lose the agent's in-progress work and context. (2) Cause another agent to be assigned the same bead, duplicating effort. (3) When the blocked agent becomes unblocked, it would continue working on a bead now assigned to someone else. 'Disconnected' means the agent is gone (crashed, shut down). 'Blocked' means the agent is present but needs help. The correct behavior for blocked agents is to escalate to the user or try to resolve the blocker, NOT to strip their work. Fix: Only unassign beads for disconnected agents. For blocked agents, send a message asking what the blocker is and/or escalate to the user after a timeout (e.g. 30 minutes blocked without progress).
title: Bug: Work-rebalancer should not unassign beads from blocked agents

2. announce: Backend broadcasts type='message' with metadata={announcementType, beadId}. Frontend AnnouncementBanner filters on metadata.type==='announcement' which is NEVER in the metadata. Announcements will never display.

Fix options:
A) Backend: Add metadata.type field to broadcasts (metadata: { type: 'agent_status', task, beadId } for status, metadata: { type: 'announcement', announcementType, beadId } for announce)
B) Frontend: Change filters to match actual broadcast shapes (check top-level type==='typing' + metadata.task for status, check top-level type==='message' + metadata.announcementType for announcements)

Tests pass because they mock the expected shape directly, masking this integration gap.

description: The parser handles single-level blockquotes (> text) but nested blockquotes (>> text or > > text) are not supported. The parser only strips one '> ' prefix. A second level of '>' would be left as literal text in the quote content. Real spec files occasionally use nested blockquotes. The recursive parse call on blockquote content does re-parse, but the inner '>' detection won't fire because the prefix is already stripped only once.

description:
The DELETE /api/scenes/:id endpoint removes the scene but does not renumber remaining scenes' order_index values on the backend. The iOS reducer correctly re-indexes locally, but the server state will have gaps (e.g., 0, 1, 3 after deleting scene 2). This causes order inconsistencies on reload and breaks the "remaining scenes renumber correctly" acceptance criterion for US2.

Recommendation: After deleting a scene, re-index all remaining scenes for that storyboard in a transaction: UPDATE scenes SET order_index = (sequential) WHERE storyboard_id = ? ORDER BY order_index.
title: Product: Scene delete does not renumber remaining scenes on server

description: Hard-stopping sliders at the budget limit creates a frustrating UX where users hit an invisible wall. Instead: (1) Allow sliders to move freely until the budget is 80% full (80 points). (2) From 80-100 points, the budget gauge transitions to amber and shows 'NEARING LIMIT'. (3) At exactly 100 points, the gauge turns bright amber and shows 'BUDGET ALLOCATED'. (4) Above 100 points, the gauge turns RED and the save/create button becomes disabled with a pulsing error state. The budget-exceeded state shows 'OVER BUDGET BY X' in red monospace text and the save button reads 'REDUCE X POINTS'. This gives users feedback and agency rather than silently clamping their input. Server-side validation remains the hard enforcement boundary.
title: Design: Soft-cap budget enforcement with amber warning zone

description: Markdown image syntax \![alt text](url) will be parsed by the link parser as a regular link with the text "]" being rejected (or the \! being orphaned text). The [ after \! triggers tryParseLink which will either succeed and render as a text link (wrong) or fail and leave raw syntax visible. title: Image syntax \![alt](url) renders as broken link

close_reason: Fixed: formatFileSize uses floating-point division for accurate small KB display
description: In FileBrowserView.swift, formatFileSize() uses integer division: 'bytes / 1024'. This is correct Swift integer division. However, for 1024 bytes exactly it shows '1 KB' which is fine. But notice the boundary: files < 1024 show 'N B' and files >= 1024 show 'N KB'. A 1500-byte file shows '1 KB' (1500/1024 = 1 in integer division). This is acceptable but imprecise. More critically, the MB formatting uses String(format: '%.1f MB', Double(bytes) / (1024 * 1024)) which could show '0.0 MB' for a file that is 1 MB (1048576 bytes exactly) since the condition is bytes < 1024*1024 which would be false at exactly 1048576. Actually wait — at 1048576 the condition 'bytes < 1024 * 1024' is false so it falls through to MB formatting: 1048576.0 / 1048576.0 = 1.0 MB. This is fine. However, there is no GB tier. A 2GB file (if ever encountered) would show '2048.0 MB' which is ugly but not a crash. Low priority.
title: Bug: formatFileSize shows '0 KB' for files between 1024 and 2047 bytes

description: After running for a few minutes, bd list commands crash with nil pointer dereference in dolt's DoltDB.SetCrashOnFatalError. Exit code 2, SIGSEGV. Likely caused by concurrent bd processes accessing the dolt database simultaneously. The backend spawns multiple bd commands (list epics, list tasks, etc.) in parallel, and dolt's SQL engine doesn't handle concurrent connections to the same database safely. Need to investigate and add serialization or connection pooling on the adjutant side.

description:
In scene-splitter.js, the OpenAI API call uses `response_format: { type: 'json_object' }`. OpenAI's json_object mode guarantees valid JSON but always returns a JSON object (not an array). So the LLM will always wrap the scenes in an object key. The code handles this with:

    const scenes = Array.isArray(parsed) ? parsed : parsed.scenes || parsed.data || parsed.result;

But if the model uses a different key (e.g., `parsed.storyboard`, `parsed.items`, `parsed.output`), this extraction fails and throws "LLM did not return a valid array of scenes." The fallback key list should be more comprehensive, or the code should search for the first array-valued property in the parsed object.

Files: backend/src/services/scene-splitter.js (line 84)
title: Bug: LLM response_format json_object may not return an array — scenes extraction fragile

close_reason: Fixed: printSummary uses arithmetic check instead of ?? falsy
description:
In cli/lib/output.ts:56 and :68, the conditional uses ?? (nullish coalescing) instead of || (logical OR):

  if (counts['pass'] ?? counts['created']) { ... }
  if (counts['skip'] ?? counts['skipped']) { ... }

This is wrong because:
- If counts['pass'] is 0 (falsy but not null/undefined), ?? treats it as a valid value and the condition is 0 (falsy), so the line won't show
- BUT 0 is the default from the loop, so if 'pass' key exists in counts it will be >= 1
- The REAL bug: if counts['pass'] is undefined but counts['created'] has a value, the ?? works correctly. But if counts['pass'] is 0 somehow, it short-circuits and the created count is ignored.

Actually the deeper issue: counts['pass'] can only be set if there ARE pass results (the loop only sets it when encountered), so undefined ?? counts['created'] works. But this is fragile and confusing. Should use || for clarity, or better yet just check both counts explicitly.

File: cli/lib/output.ts:56, 68
Severity: Low - works by accident but confusing code
title: QA: printSummary has logic bug in pass/skip count display

close_reason: Fixed: TabContent now renders only selected tab body, unvisited tabs get no content
description:
TabContent.body uses:
  ForEach(AppTab.allCases) { tab in
      NavigationStack(path: coordinator.pathBinding(for: tab)) {
          tabView(for: tab)
      }
      .opacity(selectedTab == tab ? 1 : 0)
      .allowsHitTesting(selectedTab == tab)
  }

This creates ALL 8 tab views at once (overview, chat, crew, projects, beads, timeline, proposals, settings), wrapping each in a NavigationStack. Only the selected tab is visible (.opacity(1)).

While SwiftUI may optimize rendering for hidden views, each view's init() still runs:
- SwarmOverviewView() init
- ChatView(apiClient:) init
- AgentListView(apiClient:, onSelectMember:) init
- ProjectsListView(apiClient:, onSelectProject:) init
- BeadsListView() init
- TimelineView() init
- ProposalsView() init
- SettingsView() init

Each of these likely creates @StateObject ViewModels that may trigger their own initialization chains (fetching data, setting up observers, etc.).

The .animation(nil, value: selectedTab) prevents animation on tab switches, but the eager instantiation of all 8 views increases the time-to-first-meaningful-paint significantly.

Impact: Estimated 100-300ms of unnecessary view + ViewModel initialization on first render.

Suggested fix: Use lazy instantiation - only create tab views when they're first selected. Use a dictionary to cache created views. Or use SwiftUI's built-in TabView which handles lazy rendering.
title: Bug: MainTabView instantiates ALL 8 tab views eagerly via ForEach in TabContent

description:
getStatusProvider().getStatus() returns SystemStatus (status-provider.ts:67-84) which has fields: powerState, powerCapabilities, workspace, operator, infrastructure?, rigs, agents, fetchedAt.

But DashboardResponse.status.data is typed as GastownStatus (types/index.ts:145-169) which has completely different fields: powerState, town: { name, root }, operator: { name, email, unreadMail }, infrastructure: { mayor, deacon, daemon }, rigs, fetchedAt.

The `as DashboardResponse["status"]["data"]` cast on dashboard-service.ts line 207 hides this structural mismatch. At runtime, the frontend receives SystemStatus data but TypeScript thinks it's GastownStatus.

Frontend code accessing status.data.town, status.data.operator.email, status.data.infrastructure.mayor will get undefined at runtime.

The cast also violates code style rule: "Type assertions (as) require a comment explaining why it's safe."

Fix: Either use SystemStatus in the DashboardResponse type, or transform SystemStatus to GastownStatus in the service.
title: Bug: SystemStatus vs GastownStatus type mismatch in dashboard status section

description:
Follow-up to adj-zm2fh (P0 OOM crisis — now resolved/closed). The P0 leak rate was cut ~11× (from ~280 MB/hr to ~25 MB/hr steady-state, verified from /tmp/adj-mem/poll.log over an 18h window on PID 53870, 155MB→594MB). With dev-backend.sh auto-restart + tsx watch hot-reload, the OOM runway moved from ~14-46h to ~6 days, so the operational crisis is over. This bead tracks the REMAINING work that did not ship under the P0.

## Residual leak (~25 MB/hr, still present)
The dominant amplifier fixed under adj-zm2fh was the cost-tracker `session:cost` emit cascade (commit 5a754046: suppress emit on no-op cost updates). The remaining drip is most likely:
- EventBus.emit() fires TWO inner emits per event (named + `*` wildcard) regardless of listener count.
- AdjutantCore.onAny() → for-each-behavior `behavior.act().catch(...)` allocates a Promise + .catch closure per behavior per event, even for synchronous behaviors.
Next step: heap-diff two snapshots (preserved at /tmp/adj-zm2fh-snapshots/) to confirm the dominant retainer, then either avoid per-behavior Promise allocation for sync behaviors or reduce the wildcard double-emit overhead.

## Un-done acceptance criteria carried over from adj-zm2fh
These were in the original P0 AC but were NOT the measured root cause, so they were never implemented:

### Timeline events
- [ ] Pruning runs on a recurring interval (default every 60min, configurable via env) — currently prunes only on startup
- [ ] Configurable retention window (default 7 days)
- [ ] Bounded-batch SQL DELETE so cleanup can't OOM itself
- [ ] Test: 10k events older than retention → deleted on next interval fire
- [ ] Telemetry: events_pruned_total counter, last_prune_ts, events_remaining_after_prune gauges

### Streaming bridge buffers
- [ ] Per-session buffer hard cap (default 5MB or 10k lines, configurable)
- [ ] FIFO eviction when cap hit (drop oldest, keep newest)

## Forensics preserved
- /tmp/adj-zm2fh-snapshots/ — 3 pre-OOM heap snapshots
- /tmp/adj-mem/poll.log — full RSS trend log
- /tmp/adj-histogram-3.json — analyzed retainers from snapshot #3
title: Residual backend memory leak (~25 MB/hr) + un-done OOM hardening (timeline prune interval, streaming buffer caps)

description:
In dashboard-service.ts lines 189-193, listBeads() is called with limit: DASHBOARD_BEAD_LIMIT (5). The CLI respects this limit (beads-repository.ts:838-840), returning at most 5 items. Then toBeadCategory() (line 64) computes totalCount = result.data.length, which will never exceed 5 in production, even if there are hundreds of beads.

The unit test (line 267) mocks listBeads to return 10 items WITHOUT respecting the limit parameter, so the test asserts totalCount=10 and passes — but this doesn't reflect production behavior where totalCount would be 5.

Fix: Either remove limit from listBeads calls and only limit via .slice() in toBeadCategory, or have listBeads also return a separate totalCount field that's computed before slicing.

Spec violated: FR-004 — totalCount should reflect the actual number of beads, not the sliced subset.
title: Bug: Beads totalCount capped at 5 in production (limit passed to CLI)

description:
In frontend/src/components/beads/BeadsList.tsx line 311-341, the useEffect that fetches per-bead costs depends on `[searchedBeads]`. Since `searchedBeads` is a useMemo that produces a new array reference every time the `beads` polling data changes (every 30 seconds via usePolling), the cost-fetching effect fires every 30 seconds, making up to 20 parallel fetchBeadCost API calls each time.

This is an N+1 query amplification: the beads list polls every 30s, and each poll triggers 20 individual cost API calls. At scale, this means 40+ API calls per minute just from the BeadsList component.

Fix options:
1. Add a separate polling interval for costs (e.g., 60s) decoupled from the bead list refresh
2. Use a stable dependency (e.g., a serialized list of active bead IDs) instead of the full searchedBeads array
3. Add a batch cost endpoint that accepts multiple bead IDs in one request
title: Bug: BeadsList cost-fetch useEffect re-fires every 30s poll cycle (N+1 amplification)

close_reason: Accepted: workspaceType is stored in registry for future use. Current spawner uses swarm mode which delegates workspace setup to Claude Code --worktree. Full worktree creation in LifecycleManager is deferred to a future improvement.
description:
The spec for adj-052.2.3 (Agent Spawner behavior) requires spawned agents to use worktree isolation (mandatory per project rules and CLAUDE.md). However, LifecycleManager.createSession() (lifecycle-manager.ts:106-208) accepts a `workspaceType` parameter but never acts on it. It stores the value in the registry (line 151) but never calls `git worktree add` or sets up a worktree directory.

Only swarm-service.ts (line 132-138) actually creates worktrees via `git worktree add`. If the agent-spawner behavior calls `getSessionBridge().lifecycle.createSession({ workspaceType: 'worktree' })` as the spec says, the spawned agent will run in the main project directory despite the registry claiming it's in a worktree.

**Impact**: P1 — spawned agents will share the main working directory, causing the exact silent file overwrite problem documented in adj-osic. This directly contradicts the mandatory worktree isolation rule.

**Fix**: Either (a) LifecycleManager.createSession must handle worktree creation when workspaceType=worktree, or (b) the agent-spawner behavior must create the worktree itself before calling createSession with the worktree path as projectPath, or (c) use swarm-service.ts to spawn agents. The cleanest approach is (a): add worktree setup to createSession.

**Files**: backend/src/services/lifecycle-manager.ts (createSession method), backend/src/services/swarm-service.ts (reference implementation)
title: Bug: LifecycleManager.createSession ignores workspaceType — no worktree creation

description: BeadInfo.createdDate, BeadInfo.updatedDate, BeadDetail.createdDate/updatedDate/closedDate, Message.date, and Proposal.createdDate all allocate 2 new ISO8601DateFormatter instances per call (one with fractionalSeconds, one without as fallback). These computed properties are called during sort operations in DataSyncService.fetchBeads (N*logN calls), during view rendering of bead/epic lists, and during timestamp formatting. With 100+ beads, a single sort creates ~1400 formatter allocations. Fix: use static shared formatters like TimelineEvent and PersistentMessage already do. Files: ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Bead.swift:61-73,131-151, ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Message.swift:67-71, ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Proposal.swift:57-61
title: Bug: ISO8601DateFormatter allocation storm in AdjutantKit model computed properties

description: SwarmOverviewView.formatChatTimestamp (line 638-665) and DashboardView.formatChatTimestamp (line 73-102) both allocate new ISO8601DateFormatter and DateFormatter instances on every call. These functions are called per-row for every task and epic with timestamps in the overview/dashboard views. On a dashboard showing 20 tasks + 10 epics, that is 30 formatter pair allocations per render. Fix: extract to a shared DateFormatting utility with static cached formatters. Files: ios/Adjutant/Features/SwarmOverview/SwarmOverviewView.swift:638-665, ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardView.swift:73-102
title: Bug: ISO8601DateFormatter + DateFormatter allocation in formatChatTimestamp

description: ContentView.task{} at line 46 calls DataSyncService.shared.start() synchronously. AdjutantApp.onChange(scenePhase: .active) at line 28 also calls DataSyncService.shared.start(). The .active scene phase fires during the first launch, and .task{} also fires -- both trigger start(). Although start() has a hasStarted guard, there is a race window where both code paths fire within the same RunLoop cycle, and additionally the DataSyncService.shared.start() in ContentView line 46 runs SYNCHRONOUSLY inside .task{}, blocking the async context. The duplicate start also triggers loadFromCache() which accesses ResponseCache properties. Fix: Remove the start() call from ContentView.task{} since AdjutantApp.onChange handles it. Files: ios/Adjutant/App/ContentView.swift:46, ios/Adjutant/App/AdjutantApp.swift:28
title: Bug: Duplicate DataSyncService.start() on app launch

description: CRTGlowModifier.body() applies 3 .shadow() modifiers (line 373-375 of CRTTheme.swift). In the CRTTabBar, each CRTTabBarItem applies .crtGlow() twice -- once on the icon (line 215-219 of MainTabView.swift) and once on the label (line 233-238). For the selected tab, that is 6 GPU shadow render passes per tab item. With 8 tabs visible, the initial render requires at least 6 shadow passes for the selected tab + the tabBarBackground shadow (line 192) = 7 shadow render passes minimum. Each .shadow() is a separate offscreen render pass in Core Animation. On first launch, this compounds with view layout to add measurable delay. Fix: Consolidate to a single .shadow() with combined radius, or conditionally skip for initial render. Files: ios/Adjutant/Sources/UI/Theme/CRTTheme.swift:370-380, ios/Adjutant/Core/Navigation/MainTabView.swift:207-246
title: Bug: CRTGlowModifier stacks 3 shadow passes per application

description: NoiseOverlay in CRTEffects.swift (line 222-244) uses Timer.scheduledTimer with 0.1s interval to update a phase counter. On each tick, the entire Canvas redraws with random noise pixels (line 223: size.width * size.height * 0.001 random CGFloat generations + fill operations). This fires 10 times per second on the main RunLoop, consuming CPU and blocking UI updates during launch. The .id(phase) modifier on line 232 forces a complete Canvas recreation each tick. Combined with FlickerEffect's 0.15s timer (line 116), this creates constant main-thread pressure during startup. Fix: Increase NoiseOverlay interval to 0.5s or use a static pre-rendered noise texture. Files: ios/Adjutant/Theme/CRTEffects.swift:222-244, ios/Adjutant/Theme/CRTEffects.swift:116
title: Bug: NoiseOverlay Timer fires every 100ms with full Canvas redraw

description: BeadsSectionView.relativeTime (line 148-167) creates a new ISO8601DateFormatter on every call. This function is called once per bead row in the overview. close_reason: Fixed: simplified escalation using state meta — warn first, escalate on next sweep if still idle
description:
The spec for adj-052.2.4 says: 'If agent doesn't respond within 5 minutes, escalate to user.' However, there's no existing mechanism to track whether an agent responded to a shutdown request. The current communication infrastructure is fire-and-forget:

1. comm.messageAgent() inserts a message into SQLite and broadcasts via WebSocket — but there's no acknowledgement/read-receipt system.
2. The MCP mark_read tool exists but only sets a flag on messages — it doesn't emit an event the decommissioner can subscribe to.
3. There's no 'shutdown_accepted' or 'shutdown_rejected' event in the EventBus.

The decommissioner would need to:
- Start a timer (setTimeout) after sending the shutdown request
- Subscribe to some event indicating the agent acknowledged the request  
- Clear the timer if acknowledgement received
- Escalate if timer expires

But the only signal that an agent shut down is `mcp:agent_disconnected`, which fires when the MCP SSE connection drops. If the agent ignores the message but stays connected, the decommissioner has no way to know the agent actively refused vs. simply didn't see the message.

**Impact**: P2 — the escalation feature cannot work as designed without a response-tracking mechanism. Naive implementation (just wait 5 min then check if agent is still idle) could false-positive if the agent got work assigned in the interim.

**Fix options**: 
(a) Simple: after 5 minutes, check if agent is still idle AND still connected. If yes, escalate. This doesn't track the response but covers the main case.
(b) Better: add a `shutdown:requested` / `shutdown:acknowledged` event pair to EventBus and have the decommissioner subscribe.
(c) Use the existing `agent:status_changed` to 'done' as implicit acknowledgement.

Recommend option (a) for Phase 3 MVP, with (b) as a later enhancement.

**Files**: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-decommissioner.ts (to be created), backend/src/services/event-bus.ts (may need new events)
title: Bug: Decommissioner escalation timer lacks response-tracking mechanism

However, agent-data.ts utility functions (parseAgentFields, addressToIdentity) may still be needed — move genuinely useful ones to a general utils file.

While Math.min/max make this safe from SQL injection (always produces a number), it is inconsistent with the project's parameterized query pattern used everywhere else (e.g., getRecentRetrospectives uses LIMIT ?). This also means query.limit is not added to the params array. The inconsistency makes auditing harder and violates the principle of always using parameterized queries. Low severity since Math operations guarantee numeric output, but should be fixed for consistency.
title: Bug: queryLearnings uses string interpolation for LIMIT clause instead of parameterized query

close_reason: Deferred: CASCADE delete orphaned assets is a broader concern, not specific to canvas
description:
When a canvas is deleted, CASCADE deletes propagate correctly through canvases -> canvas_layers -> layer_versions and canvases -> global_passes. However, layer_versions.asset_id uses ON DELETE SET NULL (migration line 38), meaning the assets table retains orphaned asset records (and their files on disk) after canvas deletion.

Similarly, global_passes.source_asset_id and result_asset_id use ON DELETE SET NULL (lines 57-58). Deleting a canvas leaves behind all generated image assets with no parent reference, consuming storage indefinitely.

Files: backend/src/db/migrations/006_canvases.js:38, 57-58
title: [QA] CASCADE delete on canvas does not clean up orphaned assets

description:
BackgroundTaskService.registerBackgroundTasks() registers handlers with forced downcasts:

  BGTaskScheduler.shared.register(...) { [weak self] task in
      Task { @MainActor in
          await self?.handleAppRefresh(task: task as! BGAppRefreshTask)
      }
  }

  BGTaskScheduler.shared.register(...) { [weak self] task in
      Task { @MainActor in
          await self?.handleBackgroundProcessing(task: task as! BGProcessingTask)
      }
  }

If the system passes a different BGTask subclass (unlikely but possible in future iOS versions), this will crash the app with a forced unwrap failure.

Additionally, these handlers spawn Tasks with @MainActor isolation. If the system calls the handler on a background thread (which BGTaskScheduler does), the Task creation itself is fine, but the actual work is deferred to the main actor. If the main thread is busy (e.g., during a synchronous view update), the background task handling is delayed until the main actor is available.

Impact: Potential crash from forced downcast (low probability) + potential delay in background task handling if main thread is busy.

Suggested fix: Use conditional downcast (as?) with error logging instead of forced downcast (as!).
title: Bug: BackgroundTaskService.registerBackgroundTasks uses forced downcast (as!) in handler

description: In messaging.ts:32, the send_message tool extracts sessionId via unsafe cast: (extra as Record<string, unknown>).sessionId as string. While the server-side getAgentBySession lookup prevents client-supplied agentId from being used, the pattern is fragile. If the MCP SDK changes how extra is shaped, or if a transport bug allows client-controlled sessionId, an agent could impersonate another. The status.ts tools use resolveAgent() properly which accesses extra.sessionId directly. Messaging tools should use the same resolveAgent pattern for consistency and safety.
title: Security: Agent identity spoofing via sessionId casting in messaging.ts

description: In backend/src/types/beads.ts:38, GraphNodeSchema defines source as z.string().optional(). However, the getBeadsGraph() function at beads-service.ts:1453 always calls prefixToSource(issue.id) which always returns a string (never undefined). The schema should use z.string() (required) to match the actual implementation, making the contract accurate. The data-model.md also defines source as string (required), not string | undefined.
title: Bug: GraphNodeSchema source field is optional but service always provides it

close_reason: Fixed: checkAndDeductCredits helper now decrements balance
description:
All generation endpoints (generate-base, generate element, mutate, global pass) in backend/src/routes/canvases.js check credit balance but never actually deduct credits. The credit check reads `const account = await fastify.db('credit_accounts').first()` and compares balance, but there is no `decrement` or `update` call to reduce the balance after the generation is enqueued. This means users can generate unlimited content as long as they have >= 1 credit.

FR-007 requires: "System MUST deduct 1 credit per generation, mutation, or global pass."

Files: backend/src/routes/canvases.js lines 257-268 (generate-base), 352-360 (generate element), 409-418 (mutate), 461-470 (global pass).

Additionally, the credit check is not atomic — it reads balance then enqueues work without a transaction, so concurrent requests could race past the check before any deduction occurs (if deduction were added).
title: [QA] Credit deduction is checked but never actually deducted

close_reason: Deferred P2: shared state is by design for MCP server singleton pattern
description: In mcp-server.ts, getMcpServer() creates a single McpServer instance, and connectAgent() calls server.connect(transport) for each new agent. The MCP SDK's McpServer.connect() may not be designed for multi-transport use -- the @modelcontextprotocol/sdk SSE transport docs suggest one server per transport. If the SDK internally routes tool calls to the wrong transport or clobbers state on each connect(), agents may receive each other's responses. The test mocks hide this because they don't test actual SDK routing. Verify with the MCP SDK docs whether a single McpServer can safely serve multiple SSEServerTransports, and add an integration test that connects two agents and verifies tool calls route correctly.
title: Refactor: MCP server singleton with multiple transports creates shared state issue

close_reason: Theoretical — server always returns all three sub-fields within beads section. DashboardSection<T> wrapper handles full-section failures.
description: The Swift DashboardSection<T> struct declares 'let data: T?' (optional), which is correct. However, when the backend's wrapResult helper returns a successful section it produces '{ data: <value> }' — never explicitly setting 'data: null'. When the section fails it produces '{ data: null, error: '...' }'. Swift's JSONDecoder will decode a missing 'data' key as nil for an Optional — this is fine. But there is an edge case: DashboardBeadsData.inProgress, DashboardBeadsData.open, and DashboardBeadsData.closed are all non-optional (required fields). If the backend ever returns a malformed beads section where one of these sub-fields is absent (e.g., only inProgress fails), the entire DashboardResponse decoding will throw, falling into the catch block and showing stale data with no error message. The whole response fails, defeating the per-section isolation. This is a latent risk rather than a current bug — but it will manifest if the backend schema drifts. File: ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Dashboard.swift lines 33-43 (DashboardBeadsData); backend/src/services/dashboard-service.ts wrapBeadsResult().
title: Dashboard batch: DashboardSection<T> requires data to always be present but backend can omit it

description: The act() method guards with if(\!lastReviewAt) -> startup, else if(\!lastWeeklyReviewAt) -> weekly, else skip. Once both meta keys are set after the first startup and first weekly review, the behavior permanently skips all subsequent events and cron ticks. The weekly review never runs again. The schedule '0 0 * * 1' (Monday midnight) is meaningless because the guard prevents re-execution. Fix: the weekly review guard should check if enough time has elapsed since last_weekly_review_at (e.g., >= 7 days), not just whether the key exists. File: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/memory-reviewer.ts lines 62-78

description:
ResponseCache stores chatMessages as an in-memory array:
  private(set) var chatMessages: [PersistentMessage] = []
  private var chatMessagesByAgent: [String: [PersistentMessage]] = [:]

While persistence limits to 50 messages (maxPersistedMessages), the in-memory cache has NO limit. If a chat session generates 1000+ messages:
1. chatMessages grows unbounded
2. chatMessagesByAgent stores per-agent copies (potential duplication)
3. Memory usage scales linearly with message count
4. No eviction policy

On devices with limited memory (older iPhones), this can trigger memory pressure warnings or even termination by the system.

Additionally, updateChatMessages (both variants) creates copies of the entire array for persistence. With large arrays, the copy + JSON encode in performPersist can cause memory spikes.

Impact: Memory growth proportional to chat history, potential OOM on long sessions.

Suggested fix:
- Add a maxCachedMessages limit (e.g., 200) to in-memory cache
- Implement FIFO eviction when limit is reached
- Consider using a ring buffer or sliding window approach
title: Bug: ResponseCache.chatMessages has no size limit and can grow unbounded in memory

close_reason: Fixed: loadMore finds oldest by createdAt
description: In useChatMessages.ts loadMore(), line 121: const oldestMessage = messages[0]. The hook fetches messages ordered by created_at DESC (newest first from the API). So messages[0] is actually the NEWEST message, not the oldest. The loadMore function then passes this as beforeId, which means it requests messages before the newest message -- effectively re-fetching the same page minus one message. The correct oldest message would be messages[messages.length - 1]. However, since the REST API returns items in DESC order and the hook stores them as-is, this bug means pagination will appear to work (it gets older messages because of the descending cursor) but the cursor position is wrong, potentially missing messages or returning duplicates.
title: Refactor: useChatMessages loadMore uses messages[0] as oldest but messages are DESC-sorted

The getConnectedAgents() will return phantom agents. Add a heartbeat mechanism or periodic cleanup that checks if transports are still alive, and remove stale entries. Also consider adding a max-connections guard.

The CRTGlowModifier, PhosphorGlow, TextGlow, and PulsingGlow modifiers in CRTEffects.swift all apply shadow effects unconditionally. CRTText always applies crtGlow regardless of theme. In Document mode, users would see scanlines and phosphor glow on a white background — completely inappropriate. FIX: (1) CRTScreenContainer should check theme.crtEffectsEnabled before rendering overlays. (2) CRTGlowModifier, PhosphorGlow, TextGlow, PulsingGlow should return content unmodified when crtEffectsEnabled is false. (3) CRTText should skip glow in Document mode.

description:
Per Apple documentation, BGTaskScheduler.register(forTaskWithIdentifier:using:) MUST be called before the end of application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:). The current code calls it indirectly via BackgroundTaskService.shared.registerBackgroundTasks().

This is correct. However, BackgroundTaskService.shared triggers the singleton init, which calls loadLastRefreshDate() (a UserDefaults read). If this read is slow or if the init cascade from BackgroundTaskService.shared triggers further initialization (it doesn't currently, but adding dependencies later could), there's a risk of exceeding the didFinishLaunchingWithOptions time budget.

More critically: registerBackgroundTasks() is called on the main thread. The BGTaskScheduler.register closures capture [weak self] and create Tasks with @MainActor isolation. If iOS delivers a pending background task immediately after registration (before didFinishLaunchingWithOptions returns), the Task will be queued but cannot execute until the main actor is available. The BGTask might expire before the handler runs.

The expiration handler also uses Task { @MainActor in ... }, meaning expiration handling is also delayed by main actor contention.

Impact: Low probability but could cause background task expiration before handling begins.

Suggested fix: Consider dispatching background task handlers to a background queue instead of @MainActor, and only hop to main actor for specific state updates.
title: Bug: AppDelegate.registerBackgroundTasks called before BGTaskScheduler app launch handler window closes

description:
In `backend/src/services/question-service.ts` lines 206-207:

```typescript
const isAlways = urgencyAlwaysPushes(question.urgency);
const pushEnabled = isAlways ? isAPNsConfigured() : isAPNsConfigured();
```

Both branches of the ternary are identical — `isAPNsConfigured()` is called regardless of `isAlways`. The `urgencyAlwaysPushes()` function and the `ALWAYS_PUSH_URGENCIES` constant are computed but their result is never used in the control flow. This is dead code.

**Effect:** normal/low urgency questions push whenever APNS is configured — the same behaviour as blocking/high. The spec states "Blocking/high always push; normal/low respect existing notification prefs" (spec.md line 79). The plan comment acknowledges the pref API doesn't exist yet and says "treat not configured as the proxy gate", but the implementation never actually differentiates. The `urgencyAlwaysPushes` abstraction is entirely useless as written.

**Expected:** Either (a) the ternary is removed and replaced with the simpler `const pushEnabled = isAPNsConfigured()` with a comment explaining why normal/low also push today, OR (b) when the pref API lands, the gating will be wired in — but the dead code in its current form silently breaks the invariant and will mislead the engineer who implements the pref gate.

**Missing test:** There is no test for "normal urgency when APNS IS configured — does it push?" The existing test only covers "normal when NOT configured = no push". If the intent is to push for all urgencies when configured, that should be explicitly tested and the dead ternary removed.

Files: `backend/src/services/question-service.ts` lines 124-128, 206-207.
title: QA: APNS urgency gating is dead code — normal/low always push same as blocking/high

close_reason: Fixed: both work-assigner and work-rebalancer use updateBead() from beads-mutations.ts
description: The work-assigner will call bd update <id> --assignee=<agent> --status=in_progress via execBd (bd-client.ts). But execBd is a raw CLI wrapper — it does NOT emit EventBus events. Events are only emitted in two places: (1) beads-mutations.ts updateBead() function (line 139), which is used by the REST API route. (2) mcp-tools/beads.ts update_bead tool handler, which also emits events via eventStore. If work-assigner uses execBd directly, then: (a) No bead:updated event fires, so bead-assign-notification won't send the agent a message. (b) No bead:assigned event fires (even after adj-052.1.2 adds it), so Phase 3 agent-spawner won't trigger. (c) The frontend won't get real-time WebSocket updates about the assignment. (d) The eventStore timeline won't record the assignment. The work-assigner must either: (a) Use the beads-mutations.updateBead() service function which handles event emission, or (b) Manually emit the appropriate events after a successful execBd call. Option (a) is preferred as it avoids duplicating event emission logic.
title: Bug: Work-assigner using raw execBd will not emit bead:updated or bead:assigned events

close_reason: Fixed: work-rebalancer tracks recently-rebalanced beads with 60s TTL Map
description: When work-rebalancer unassigns a bead (bd update <id> --status=open --assignee=), this triggers a bead:updated event. But bead:updated is NOT a work-assigner trigger (only bead:created, agent:status_changed, bead:closed are). So the work-assigner won't immediately pick up the orphaned bead — it waits for the next cron sweep. However, the more dangerous scenario is: (1) Agent A goes blocked. (2) Work-rebalancer unassigns Agent A's bead. (3) Work-assigner (on next tick) assigns bead to Agent B. (4) Agent A comes back from blocked to working. (5) Agent A is still working on the bead it had (it doesn't know it was unassigned). (6) Now Agent A and Agent B are both working on the same bead. There is no mechanism to tell Agent A to stop working on its old bead. The communication goes one way only — the system can assign, but cannot forcibly stop an agent's work. Fix: When work-rebalancer unassigns a bead from a blocked agent, it should also message the agent: 'Your bead X has been reassigned. Stop work on it.' For disconnected agents, this is unnecessary (they can't receive messages). Additionally, consider a delay before unassigning from blocked agents.
title: Bug: Work-rebalancer can trigger infinite reassignment loop with work-assigner
